                                        As Adopted by:
                                  Town of Paonia, Colorado
                       Resolution No. 2017-10 – Amended May 22, 2018

                                      I. Rules of Procedure

Section 1. Schedule of Meetings. Regular Board of Trustees meetings shall be held on the
second and fourth Tuesdays of each month, except on legal holidays, or as re-scheduled or
amended and posted on the agenda prior to the scheduled meeting.

Section 2. Officiating Officer. The meetings of the Board of Trustees shall be conducted by the
Mayor or, in the Mayor’s absence, the Mayor Pro-Tem. The Town Clerk or a designee of the
Board shall record the minutes of the meetings.
Section 3. Time of Meetings. Regular meetings of the Board of Trustees shall begin at 6:30
p.m. or as scheduled and posted on the agenda. Board Members shall be called to order by the
Mayor. The meetings shall open with the presiding officer leading the Board in the Pledge of
Allegiance. The Town Clerk shall then proceed to call the roll, note the absences and
announce whether a quorum is present. Regular Meetings are scheduled for three hours, and
shall be adjourned at 9:30 p.m., unless a majority of the Board votes in the affirmative to extend
the meeting, by a specific amount of time.
Section 4. Schedule of Business. If a quorum is present, the Board of Trustees shall proceed
with the business before it, which shall be conducted in the following manner. Note that all
provided times are estimated:
                (a) Roll Call - (5 minutes)
                (b) Approval of Agenda - (5 minutes)
                (c) Announcements (5 minutes)
                (d) Recognition of Visitors and Guests (10 minutes)
                (e) Consent Agenda including Approval of Prior Meeting Minutes (10
              minutes)
                (f) Mayor’s Report (10 minutes)
                (g) Staff Reports: (15 minutes)
                    (1) Town Administrator’s Report
                    (2) Public Works Reports
                    (3) Police Report
                    (4) Treasurer Report

                (h) Unfinished Business (45 minutes)
                (i) New Business (45 minutes)
                (j) Disbursements (15 minutes)
                (k) Committee Reports (15 minutes)
                (l) Adjournment
                       * This schedule of business is subject to change and amendment.
Section 5. Priority and Order of Business. Questions relative to the priority of business
and order shall be decided by the Mayor without debate, subject in all cases to an
appeal to the Board of Trustees.

Section 6. Conduct of Board Members. Town Board Members shall treat other Board
Members and the public in a civil and polite manner and shall comply with the
Standards of Conduct for Elected Officials of the Town. Board Members shall address
Town Staff and the Mayor by his/her title, other Board Members by the title of Trustee
or the appropriate honorific (i.e.: Mr., Mrs. or Ms.), and members of the public by the
appropriate honorific. Subject to the Mayor’s discretion, Board Members shall be


                                                                                             Page 3 of 614
limited to speaking two times when debating an item on the agenda. Making a motion,
asking a question or making a suggestion are not counted as speaking in a debate.

Section 7. Presentations to the Board. Items on the agenda presented by individuals,
businesses or other organizations shall be given up to 5 minutes to make a
presentation. On certain issues, presenters may be given more time, as determined by
the Mayor and Town Staff. After the presentation, Trustees shall be given the
opportunity to ask questions.

Section 8. Public Comment. After discussion of an agenda item by the Board of
Trustees has concluded, the Mayor shall open the floor for comment from members of
the public, who shall be allowed the opportunity to comment or ask questions on the
agenda item. Each member of the public wishing to address the Town Board shall be
recognized by the presiding officer before speaking. Members of the public shall speak
from the podium, stating their name, the address of their residence and any group they
are representing prior to making comment or asking a question. Comments shall be
directed to the Mayor or presiding officer, not to an individual Trustee or Town
employee. Comments or questions should be confined to the agenda item or issue(s)
under discussion. The speaker should offer factual information and refrain from
obscene language and personal attacks.

Section 9. Unacceptable Behavior. Disruptive behavior shall result in expulsion from
the meeting.

Section 10. Posting of Rules of Procedure for Paonia Board of Trustees Meetings.
These rules of procedure shall be provided in the Town Hall meeting room for each
Board of Trustees meeting so that all attendees know how the meeting will be
conducted.

II. Consent Agenda

Section 1. Use of Consent Agenda. The Mayor, working with Town Staff, shall place
items on the Consent Agenda. By using a Consent Agenda, the Board has consented
to the consideration of certain items as a group under one motion. Should a Consent
Agenda be used at a meeting, an appropriate amount of discussion time will be allowed
to review any item upon request.
Section 2. General Guidelines. Items for consent are those which usually do not require
discussion or explanation prior to action by the Board, are non-controversial and/or
similar in content, or are those items which have already been discussed or explained
and do not require further discussion or explanation. Such agenda items may include
ministerial tasks such as, but not limited to, approval of previous meeting minutes,
approval of staff reports, addressing routine correspondence, approval of liquor licenses
renewals and approval or extension of other Town licenses. Minor changes in the
minutes such as non-material Scribner errors may be made without removing the
minutes from the Consent Agenda. Should any Trustee feel there is a material error in
the minutes, they should request the minutes be removed from the Consent Agenda for
Board discussion.
Section 3. Removal of Item from Consent Agenda. One or more items may be removed
from the Consent Agenda by a timely request of any Trustee. A request is timely if
made prior to the vote on the Consent Agenda. The request does not require a second
or a vote by the Board. An item removed from the Consent Agenda will then be

                                                                                    Page 4 of 614
discussed and acted on separately either immediately following the consideration of the
Consent Agenda or placed later on the agenda, at the discretion of the Board.

III. Executive Session

Section 1. An executive session may only be called at a regular or special Board
meeting where official action may be taken by the Board, not at a work session of the
Board. To convene an executive session, the Board shall announce to the public in the
open meeting the topic to be discussed in the executive session, including specific
citation to the statute authorizing the Board to meet in an executive session and
identifying the particular matter to be discussed “in as much detail as possible without
compromising the purpose for which the executive session is authorized.” In the event
the Board plans to discuss more than one of the authorized topics in the executive
session, each should be announced, cited and described. Following the announcement
of the intent to convene an executive session, a motion must then be made and
seconded. In order to go into executive session, there must be the affirmative vote of
two thirds (2/3) of Members of the Board.

Section 2. During executive session, minutes or notes of the deliberations should not
be taken. Since meeting minutes are subject to inspection under the Colorado Open
Records Act, the keeping of minutes would defeat the private nature of executive
session. In addition, the deliberations carried out during executive session should not
be discussed outside of that session or with individuals not participating in the
session. The contexts of an executive session are to remain confidential unless a
majority of the Trustees vote to disclose the contents of the executive session.

Section 3. Once the deliberations have taken place in executive session, the Board
should reconvene in regular session to take any formal action decided upon during the
executive session. If you have questions regarding the wording of the motion or
whether any other information should be disclosed on the record, it is essential for you
to consult with the Town Attorney on these matters.


IV. Subject to Amendment

Section 1. Deviations. The Board may deviate from the procedures set forth in this
Resolution, if, in its sole discretion, such deviation is necessary under the
circumstances.
Section 2. Amendment. The Board may amend these Rules of Procedures Policy
from time to time.

                                                                       Page 8 of 614
         A Rental Agreement Between Harvester Building, LLC
                                 and
                   Juan Curiel and Claudia Moreno

         Old Cave Cafe, LLC, Landlord, and Curiel LLC also known as Rio
Bravo (Tenant,) represented by Juan Curiel and Claudia Moreno agree that:
         1. The Landlord will rent 240 Grand Avenue, top floor of Paonia,
Colorado, to the Tenant.
         2. The lease will run from April 8, 2026, to May 31,2027.
         3. The rent shall be       /month. The rent shall be due on the first of
each month and late on the 10th. If it is not paid, eviction may begin by the
 15th at the expense of the Tenant. A $25 penalty will be charged after the
 10th. The Landlord currently holds a $         deposit.
         4. The space shall be used as a restaurant with the allowable sale of
alcohol, town permitted.
         5. This lease is renewable at the option of the Tenant for the calendar
year 2025 at mutually agreed by both parties.
         6. The tenant is responsible for heating, cooling, electricity, water and
the cleaning of the corridors and bathrooms. The Landlord shall maintain the
building and systems, but not the hood or appliances used by Rio Bravo.
         7. Painting, remodeling, etc. The Tenant shall not make any changes
to the office without consulting the Landlord. The landlord has agreed to
install new carpet at his expense.
         8. The Landlord has the right to show the space to prospective buyers.
This lease shall be a condition of any sale.
         9. The Landlord shall insure the building against fire, lighting,
extended coverage, vandalism and malicious mischief.
         10. Rent checks shall be made payable to Old Cave Cafe LLC, and
either dropped in the box at Harvester Building or deposited directly into
landlord’s account at First Colorado National Bank in Paonia.

RE: Rio Bravo (Curiel LLC) Liquor License Renewal

From Daoine B <DaoineB@townofpaonia.com>
Date Wed 4/15/2026 11:31 AM
To      Ruben Santiago <RubenS@townofpaonia.com>; Matt Laiminger <mlaimingerppd@townofpaonia.com>; Nicki
        P <nickip@townofpaonia.com>; Stefen Wynn <StefenW@townofpaonia.com>
Cc      Samira Vetter <SamiraV@townofpaonia.com>


The subject property is located within C-1, the Core Commercial District which has the following intent, “It is
the intent of this District to provide for the orderly development of those commercial and business uses,
government, educational and cultural facilities that are characteristic of downtown areas and promote
comparison shopping and pedestrian activity in the core area. This District is not intended for businesses
and commercial uses that are oriented to the automobile and require extensive ground-level floor area.”
Permissible uses in the district include uses that typically serve alcohol including restaurants. From a zoning
perspective, there isn’t an issue with the use within the subject property’s zoning district.

 STAFF REPORT: LIQUOR LICENSE RENEWAL (RIO BRAVO)                                   04/22/2026



Paonia Police Department Review – Liquor License Renewal
Property: 240 Grand Avenue, Paonia, CO 81428

The Paonia Police Department reviewed calls for service at the address and spoke with the
applicant. Based on the findings and information provided by the applicant, the Paonia Police
Department recommends the renewal of the liquor license.



Relevant Information

   •   The department responded to zero calls for service at the business known as Rio Bravo in
       the previous twelve months.
   •   Applicant has made no changes to the storage location of the alcohol stored on premises.


Summary

The Paonia Police Department recommends renewal of the Rio Bravo liquor license.


*This evaluation is based on the operational information provided by the applicant at the
time of review. Any material changes to the premises layout, occupancy, access points, or
business operations may require additional review and approval by the appropriate Town
departments and regulatory authorities.
*This review is limited to the information provided by the applicant and internal data. It
does not constitute a comprehensive inspection of the premises or a certification that the
property complies with all applicable laws, regulations, or safety codes.

     Tax Check Authorization, Waiver, and Request to Release Information

i,

am signing this Tax Check Authorization, Waiver and Request to Release Information (hereinafter

“Waiver”) on behalf of
 the "Applicant/Licensee”)



to permit the Colorado Department of Revenue and any other state or local taxing authority to release
information and documentation that may otherwise be confidential, as provided below. If I am signing
this Waiver for someone other than myself, including on behalf of a business entity, I certify that I have
the authority to execute this Waiver on behalf of the Applicant/Licensee.
The Executive Director of the Colorado Department of Revenue is the State Licensing Authority, and
oversees the Colorado Liquor Enforcement Division as his or her agents, clerks, and employees. The
information and documentation obtained pursuant to this Waiver may be used in connection with the
Applicant/Licensee’s liquor license application and ongoing licensure by the state and local licensing
authorities. The Colorado Liquor Code, section 44-3-101. et seq. (“Liquor Code”), and the Colorado
Liquor Rules, 1 CCR 203-2 (“Liquor Rules”), require compliance with certain tax obligations, and set
forth the investigative, disciplinary and licensure actions the state and local licensing authorities may
take for violations of the Liquor Code and Liquor Rules, including failure to meet tax reporting and
payment obligations.
The Waiver is made pursuant to section 39-21-113(4), C.R.S., and any other law, regulation, resolution
or ordinance concerning the confidentiality of tax information, or any document, report or return filed
in connection with state or local taxes. This Waiver shall be valid until the expiration or revocation
of a license, or until both the state and local licensing authorities take final action to approve or deny
any application(s) for the renewal of the license, whichever is later. Applicant/Licensee agrees to
execute a new waiver for each subsequent licensing period in connection with the renewal of any
license, if requested.

By signing below, Applicant/Licensee requests that the Colorado Department of Revenue and any
other state or local taxing authority or agency in the possession of tax documents or information,
release information and documentation to the Colorado Liquor Enforcement Division, and is duly
authorized employees, to act as the Applicant’s/Licensee’s duly authorized representative under
section 39-21-113(4), C.R.S., solely to allow the state and local licensing authorities, and their duly
authorized employees, to investigate compliance with the Liquor Code and Liquor Rules. Applicant/
Licensee authorizes the state and local licensing authorities, their duly authorized employees, and
their legal representatives, to use the information and documentation obtained using this Waiver in any
administrative or judicial action regarding the application or license.

                                           MEMORANDUM

TO:         Region 10, Courtney Tribble
FROM:       Ashley Rodarte, Payroll and Accounting Specialist
DATE:       Friday, April 17, 2026
SUBJECT: Progress Report on Mini Grant Progress for the Town of Paonia


      1. Project Overview & Progress Summary

The Town of Paonia started progress on the Community Development Mini-Grant for the Welcome to
Paonia: Entry Beauti�ication and Cultural Expression Project on February 17, 2026. The goal of the project
is to enhance the community’s identity, improve the aesthetics of key gateways, and create a more vibrant
and welcoming environment for both citizens and visitors.

The project entailed rehabilitation of the “Welcome to Paonia” sign, a new seasonal banner program for the
downtown corridor and the fabrication and installation of public parking lot signs.

                •   Task 1:    Conduct a community art contest for manufacturing banner designs

                •   Task 2:    Commission original artist from the way�inding plan to fabricate and
                    install public parking lot signs

                •   Task 3:    Refurbishment of the “Welcome to Paonia” signs


Signi�icant progress has been made in tasks 1 and 2, with progress being established in task 3.


      2. Task Progress Details

Task 1: Conduct a community art contest for manufacturing banner designs

Research into conducting a local art contest was conducted starting February 17, 2026, along with
communication with the North Fork Valley Creative Coalition to inquire if assistance with the contest would
be applicable. Due to increasing deadlines, the Town of Paonia released the community art contest on
Monday, April 9, 2026. The art contest entailed that each artist competing was to submit two designs. There
were no stipulations on the medium or the age of the contestants. The art contest closed on Friday, April 3,
2026, with 5 contestants each submitting two designs. A virtual survey was then conducted the same day
and closes today, Friday, April 17, 2026, at 4:00 p.m. The top 3 contestants will then be rewarded $500.00
each and their designs reproduced into banners. These banners will be produced through Craftsmen
Industries.




                                                                                                   Page 46 of 614
                                        Invoices Applied to Task 1

There are no current invoices applicable for this task, the budget for this task is $5,000.00 for the banners,
$1,500.00 for the brackets and $1,500.00 for artist compensation for the three contest winners.

                                     Total Expended on Task 1: $0.00

                                  Total Budgeted on Task 1: $8,000.00

                                     Task Completion Estimate 75%




Task 2: Commission original artist from the way�inding plan to fabricate and install public parking
lot signs

An interview with Ira Houseweart was established to commission the fabrication and installation of custom
public parking lot signs designed from the established Paonia Creative District Signage & Way�inding Plan.
Through the conversation with Ira Houseweart, the idea of custom sculpted features for the signs would be
a great way of expressing a creative interpretation of the community culture. An employee for the Town of
Paonia, Ashley Rodarte, volunteered to make these features at no cost to the Town of Paonia, the only cost
was for materials. The �irst installment to start the public parking lot signs was issued March 27, 2026.

                                        Invoices Applied to Task 2

   •   March 2026 Invoice 1539 $3,750.00

   •   April 2026 Invoice 1CKK-CDKG-VK9G $74.83


                                  Total Expended on Task 2: $3,824.83

                                  Total Budgeted on Task 2: $7,500.00

                                     Task Completion Estimate 50%




Task 3: Refurbishment of the “Welcome to Paonia” signs

A trip was taken to the site of the signs to establish the current condition and the work it would take to
refurbish the two signs. The decision was made that the signs could be replicated with new material with a
local artist and make the two signs more cohesive and easier to maintain. Meetings with the North Fork
Valley Creative Coalition were held to establish what was expected for the signs. This has caused a delay in
acquiring an artist(s) to achieve this task, along with possible budget constraints for artist compensation.
Currently the Alcon Lighting solar panel lights which are Paonia Dark Skies compatible have been ordered
and are ready for installation once signs are completed.




                                                                                                    Page 47 of 614
                                       Invoices Applied to Task 3

    •   March 2026 Invoice FEB 2026 STATEMENT $4,194.60


                                   Total Expended on Task 3: $4,194.60

                                   Total Budgeted on Task 3: $7,000.00

                                      Task Completion Estimate 45%

    3. Remaining Tasks for Completion

Finalization of 3 contest winners to submit the purchase of 30 banners and brackets. Once the fabrication
of the public parking lot signs is completed, the �inal payment will be issued to install the signs in the
designated locations. Lastly, the selection of an artist(s) to refurbish the “Welcome to Paonia” signs and
installation of solar panel lights will bring the whole project to completion.




In Public Service,




Ashley Rodarte
Paonia Payroll and Accounting Specialist




                                                                                                Page 48 of 614
                                            TOWN OF PAONIA
                                       BOARD OF TRUSTEES MEETING
                                             STAFF REPORT



AGENDA ITEM:    Consideration of a request for approval of a Special Use Review
                application to add an additional guest parking spot west of Unit #4 in the
                Silver Leaf Co-Housing Development from Michael Cook at the Address
                1200 3rd St, Unit 4, Paonia CO 81428.

SUBMITTED BY:   Daoine Bachran, Utility Billing & Permit Coordinator

DATE:           4-24-2026

BACKGROUND:     SVR 2025-01 is an application to add a guest parking spot along Clark
                Avenue for Unit 4 of the Silver Leaf subdivision at Clark Avenue and Third
                St.

                The proposal was initially seen by the Planning Commission on November
                3, 2025. The driveway plans at that time blocked drainage along a bioswale,
                and the driveway itself was not oriented in accordance with the Town of
                Paonia’s Municipal code. The applicant requested time to adjust the plans
                per Town staff’s recommendations, and the hearing was continued on April
                6th, 2026.

                The new driveway plans provided on 4/2/2026, in time to be included in the
                hearing’s packet, addressed most of the issues mentioned in the original
                staff report. After reviewing the additional recommendations listed in the
                new staff report for the April 6th meeting, on the day of the hearing the
                applicant provided another set of plans that were modiﬁed based on issues
                addressed in the new staff report.

                The Planning commission reviewed the revised plans from 4/2/2026, and
                the newest iteration of the plans were explained by Bowman engineering.

                Mayor Smith moved to recommend conditional approval of SRV 2025-01
                subject to the conditions outlined by staff, number 1 through 5, with a
                modiﬁcation to recommendation 2 that the underlying pipe can be HDPE
                or metal and ﬁnding that the application can meet the intent of Town
                standards with appropriate design modiﬁcations.




                                                                                     Page 49 of 614
The motion was seconded by Commissioner Wynn and unanimously
approved.

The conditions listed by staff for approval are as follows:

    1. Additional Approvals: The Silver Leaf HOA provides their approval
       for the project in written form.
    2. Culvert Design Modiﬁcations: Notes on the engineered plan
       showing the use of corrugated, galvanized metal with metal end
       sections for the culvert, demonstrating the pipe extends a
       minimum of 12” beyond the embankment/shoulder, and adding a
       minimum 6” of class 6 aggregate base course material over the
       culvert.
    3. Driveway Design Modiﬁcations: The addition of a minimum of 6”
       and ideally 12” of Class 6 base course beneath the driveway’s
       gravel and above the compacted subgrade and information about
       the type of gravel composing the top of the driveway.
    4. Concrete Edge Treatment or Driveway Apron: The installation of a
       reinforced concrete edge pan (minimum 6 inches wide and 8
       inches deep) OR a 3” deep and 2’ long asphalt apron at the street
       interface.
    5. Post-Construction Certiﬁcation: The provision of a certiﬁed
       engineer’s letter conﬁrming that the bioswale, vegetation, and
       roadway edge have been restored to design standards.

The plans presented on 4/23/2026 for this meeting reﬂect the following
changes:
    1. Additional Approvals: The Silver Leaf HOA has not yet provided
       their written approval for the project.
    2. Culvert Design Modiﬁcations: Have been addressed in the new
       plan set.
    3. Driveway Design Modiﬁcation: Have been addressed in the new
       plan set.
    4. Concrete Edge Treatment or Driveway Apron: Have been
       addressed in the new plan set.
    5. Post-Construction Certiﬁcation: Has been addressed in the new
       plan                                                       set.

Additionally, the new driveway plans are compliant with the 2010 ADA
Standards for Accessible Design, not including the signage and marking
that would be required for a public parking spot (which this is not), and the
potential for erosion is addressed with notes about seeding.




                                                                      Page 50 of 614
RECOMMENDATION: Staff recommends the conditional approval of SVR 2025-01 contingent on
                the written approval of the Silver Leaf HOA.


ATTACHMENT:            •   Driveway Details: Cook Residence Driveway Design 4/23/2026
                       •   Staff Report from April 6, 2026

                                       Planning Commission Meeting Minutes

RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS

Chair Bachran calls the meeting to order at 6:00 PM.

A) Roll Call
   Present:
   Chair Bachran
   Vice-Chair Doherty (via Zoom)
   Commissioner Howe
   Commissioner Smith
   Commissioner Stelter
   Commissioner Wynn

      Absent:
      Commissioner Hunter (recused)

B) Approval of Agenda

      Commissioner Stelter makes a motion to approve the agenda. Seconded by Commissioner
      Smith.

      The motion carries unanimously.

C) Approval of Minutes

      1)     Minutes: March 2, 2026 Planning Commission Meeting Minutes

      Commissioner Smith makes a motion to approve the minutes. Seconded by Commissioner
      Stelter.

      The motion carries unanimously.

D) Public Hearing

       The Planning Commission held a quasi-judicial public hearing on April 6, 2026, to consider a
       Special Use Review application submitted by Michael Cook for the addition of a guest parking
       space west of Unit #4 in the Silver Leaf Co-Housing Development at 1200 3rd Street, Unit 4.
       This matter was a continuation of the public hearing originally opened on November 3, 2025.
       Chair Bachran opens the public hearing at 6:02 PM.

       Chair Bachran confirmed required disclosures and outlined the procedures for testimony and
       deliberation. Daoine Bachran, Utilities & Permits Coordinator, presented on behalf of staff a

 April 6, 2026 Planning Commission Meeting Minutes                                                     1




                                                                                            Page 83 of 614
      detailed findings report of the application, applicable regulations, and prior concerns, noting
      that the request had been continued to allow the applicant to address issues related to drainage,
      access, and engineering design. Staff reported that revised plans substantially addressed earlier
      deficiencies through the addition of a culvert, improved grading, and updated construction
      details, but recommended conditional approval to ensure full compliance with Town standards
      and the Silver Leaf Development Agreement.

      The staff recommendation of conditional approval of SRV 2025-01 subject to the following
      minimum requirements:
      1. Additional Approvals: The Silver Leaf HOA provides their approval for the project in written
         form.
      2. Culvert Design Modifications: Notes on the engineered plan showing the use of corrugated,
         galvanized metal with metal end sections for the culvert, demonstrating the pipe extends a
         minimum of 12” beyond the embankment/shoulder, and adding a minimum 6” of class 6 aggregate
         base course material over the culvert.
      3. Driveway Design Modifications: The addition of a minimum of 6” and ideally 12” of
         Class 6 base course beneath the driveway’s gravel and above the compacted subgrade and
         information about the type of gravel composing the top of the driveway.
      4. Concrete Edge Treatment or Driveway Apron: The installation of a reinforced concrete
         edge pan (minimum 6 inches wide and 8 inches deep) OR a 3” deep and 2’ long asphalt
         apron at the street interface.
      5. Post-Construction Certification: The provision of a certified engineer’s letter confirming
         that the bioswale, vegetation, and roadway edge have been restored to design standards.
      The applicant, through legal counsel Stephanie Fanos, and Bowman engineer Jeff Ruppert,
      presented the revised proposal and explained that the request was intended to remedy access
      limitations affecting Unit #4 due to changes made during the original subdivision construction.
      Updated plans submitted at the hearing included additional modifications related to ADA
      access and drainage improvements.
      Public comment:

      •    D. Cheeseman, in-town resident and applicant neighbor, provided background on deer
           fence and pathways put in place.
      •    M. Bennett, in-town resident and former neighbor, speaks in favor of approval by the Silver
           Leaf HOA and Planning Commission.
      •    H. Griswold, in-town resident, raises concerns regarding liability and responsibility for
           maintenance.
      •    J. Bennett, in-town resident, speaks in favor of approval.
      Staff and the Town Attorney Buchner provided clarification on technical and legal questions
      raised during the public comment period.
      Chair Bachran closes the public hearing at 7:26 PM.

 April 6, 2026 Planning Commission Meeting Minutes                                                   3




                                                                                             Page 85 of 614
                            TOWN OF PAONIA
                            PLANNING COMMISSION & COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT
                            STAFF REPORT



                       REPORT OF THE PERMIT COORDINATOR
           CONTINUANCE OF APPLICATION FOR SPECIAL USE REVIEW
                           (Real Estate Parcel Number: 324505273004,
                     Commonly known as 1200 3rd St. Unit 4 Paonia, CO 81428)
                              For Public Hearing on April 6, 2026



Background
This meeting is a continuance of the November 3, 2025 review, requested by the applicant to address
problems mentioned in the staff report and to provide engineered drawings.
SRV 2025-01 Application for Special Use Review as outlined in § 16-4-40 of the Town of Paonia’s
Municipal Code of Ordinances for the Parcel Number 324506114016, the property commonly known
as: 325 Grand Avenue Paonia, CO 81428. The property is currently listed as C-1, Core Commercial
District.
In addition to the original application SRV 2025-01, requesting approval for a driveway and parking
space along Clark Avenue serving Unit 4 of the Silverleaf Townhomes, the applicant has submitted
additional letters explaining how issues were addressed and engineered plans detailing the
driveway’s construction.
The parcel is within the Silverleaf Subdivision, governed by the Silverleaf Development Agreement
(2016) and associated as-built drawings recorded with Delta County. The proposed location lies
within the subdivision’s bioswale and drainage swale system constructed for stormwater
conveyance along Clark Avenue.

General Information
Zoning and Land Use:
The property is zoned R-2, Medium Density Residential, within the Silverleaf Subdivision. All
access improvements must conform to the subdivision’s recorded plans, the Paonia Municipal Code,
and referenced Delta County standards.

Applicable Regulations:
   •   Paonia Municipal Code (PMC) Chapter 16 – Zoning, particularly § 16-6-10 through § 16-7-70
       (Off-Street Parking and Access).

   •   Paonia Municipal Code (PMC) Appendix D – Standard Construction Specifications Part III,
       specifically drainage.




                                                                                            Page 86 of 614
                         TOWN OF PAONIA
                         PLANNING COMMISSION & COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT
                         STAFF REPORT


  •   Delta County Roadway Design and Construction Standards (DCRDCS) (August 15, 2014),
      adopted by reference in the Silverleaf Development Agreement for access and drainage
      design.

  •   Silverleaf Development Agreement (2016), establishing common drainage features, shared
      maintenance responsibilities, and required conformance with approved construction plans.

  •   ADA Standards for Accessible Design (2010)

Documents Reviewed:
  •   SRV 2025-01 Application and Site Plan

  •   Revised Project Description for Special Use Review Application of Michael Cook for
      Additional Guest Parking Space

  •   Bowman’s RE: Revised Project Description for Special Use Review Application of Michael
      Cook for Additional Guest Parking Space

  •   Bowman’s Driveway Details: Cook Residence Driveway Design

  •   Silverleaf Development Agreement (2016)

  •   Delta County Roadway Standards (August 15, 2014)

  •   Chapter 16 – Zoning and Appendix D, Town of Paonia Municipal Code

  •   ADA Standards for Accessible Design (2010)

  •   Recorded Deeds for Unit 4 (2016)

  •   Silverleaf 2015 – 2017 Construction Drawings

  •   Planning Commission Minutes – November 3, 2025

  •   Planning Commission Minutes – January 14, 2016




                                                                                       Page 87 of 614
                            TOWN OF PAONIA
                            PLANNING COMMISSION & COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT
                            STAFF REPORT

Required Findings Needed to Consider a Special Use Review:
This section contains the findings of the November 3, 2025 Staff Report, followed by the response
from the applicant, and finally the current findings based on the newly submitted engineered designs.
As established in PMC §16-4-50, Site Plan Review Criteria and Performance Standards.
(a) The criteria for review and performance standards are as follows:

       (1) Compliance of the application with this Code in general.

           The proposed driveway and parking pad do not comply with the requirements of Article
           6 – Off-Street Parking and Loading.

           •   §16-6-10 requires off-street spaces to be located on the lot and unobstructed. The
               proposed pad lies within a public drainage easement, not within the private lot’s
               approved boundaries, and therefore cannot qualify as an off-street parking space.

               Applicant Response: There is existing guest parking for Silver Leaf on Clark Ave
               accessible to other units within Silver Leaf that is substantially similar to the layout
               of the proposed parking space. Unit 4 is the only unit in the Silver Leaf development
               that does not have a close, convenient, direct and accessible path to a driveway, guest
               parking area or public right-of-way.

               New Findings: The parking spot continues to lie within a public drainage easement
               and not within the private lot’s approved boundaries. However, as explained below,
               the practical use of the drainage easement has been addressed.

           •   §16-6-60(a)–(b) requires all parking areas to be unobstructed and provide lawful
               access to and from a street. The proposed location obstructs the engineered bioswale
               and lacks a legal access point.

               Applicant Response: The proposed parking is designed to provide unobstructed and
               lawful access from Clark Avenue to Unit 4. Access to Unit 4 from the existing guest
               parking area off Clark Avenue is obstructed and does not provide a safe and stable
               pedestrian access path to Unit 4, contrary to all other units adjacent to the existing
               guest parking area. Unit 4 is uniquely challenged by these obstructions.

               New Findings: The new parking location and structure addresses the bioswale
               obstruction and provides more than the required 30’ from urban intersections,
               exactly the maximum 2% grade for the 10’ approaching the street, and falls within
               the 12’ min and 18’ max width required by DCRDCS.

           •   §16-6-60(c) requires a durable surface such as asphalt or concrete. The applicant
               has not provided a surfacing plan or structural edge treatment.




                                                                                               Page 88 of 614
                 TOWN OF PAONIA
                 PLANNING COMMISSION & COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT
                 STAFF REPORT

    Applicant Response: The proposed parking space surface is durable. There are no
    parking spaces within Silver Leaf that are either asphalt or concrete, except for those
    units that have a carport and/or garage. The proposed parking space surface is
    consistent with the surface of the existing guest parking off Clark Avenue.

    New Findings: The engineered plans now contain 4” of Class 6 road base topped with
    4” of crushed gravel on compacted backfill with a gravel apron and a 3:1 shoulder
    slope. The original Silver Leaf driveway construction specified 4.75” of crushed gravel
    on top of an engineer-determined amount of road base. DCRDCS Appendix 5 Items 15
    & 16 recommend 12” of sub-base between gravel top and compacted subgrade, a 2%
    cross-grade on either side of the driveway’s centerline, and a 4’ minimum tapered
    shoulder on either side of the driveway with a maximum 2:1 grade.

    The crushed gravel, while under Silver Leaf’s minimum is to standards as is the
    driveway’s shoulder. Increasing the class 6 road base to 6” is standard, while 12”
    would be preferred. Ideally, the apron should be composed of asphalt or concrete—
    as the driveway adjoins gravel to asphalt.

•   §16-6-60(j) requires driveways and parking areas to be designed with adequate aisle
    spacing and safe approach geometry. The proposed space is angled, not
    perpendicular, and features a five-to-six-foot elevation change from the roadway
    edge, creating an unsafe entry grade.

    Applicant Response: The proposed parking space has been redesigned to eliminate
    the angled parking and includes a safe entry grade from Clark Avenue within
    acceptable elevation shifts as confirmed by a recent survey prepared by Wilmore
    Company, and complies with all Town and County driveway standards.

    New Findings: The redesign has addressed the original issue.

•   §16-6-60(h) requires landscaped drainage areas. The proposed pad would replace
    an existing bioswale with impervious surface, eliminating required stormwater
    functionality.

    Applicant Response: The drainage issues have been addressed with a culvert under
    the proposed driveway. The proposed driveway will not inhibit the function of the
    subgrade stormwater detention areas. Other existing landscaping will not be
    impacted.

    New Findings: The new design does provide the required sized 12” culvert to allow
    for drainage under the driveway. The design notes that the culvert “may be HDPE or
    galvanized metal.” PMC Appendix D Part III requires that “culverts shall be corrugated,
    galvanized metal with metal end sections.” DCRDCS §4.9.10(c) requires that the “pipe
    inlet/outlet extends a minimum of twelve (12) inches beyond the surrounding
    embankment of the Driveway. The pipe will be bedded with a minimum of 6 inches




                                                                                   Page 89 of 614
                     TOWN OF PAONIA
                     PLANNING COMMISSION & COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT
                     STAFF REPORT

       of Class 6 aggregate base course material.” The new plans do not provide 6” of class 6
       bedding over the culvert. They do, however, seem to provide the 12” extension of the
       pipe outside the graded shoulder, and they do provide for 2” over the required 6” of
       rip-rap covering the exposed ends.

(2) The compatibility of the proposed use and site plan with the character of the surrounding
    area.

   The surrounding properties within the Silverleaf Subdivision maintain consistent,
   approved access points and unified drainage infrastructure. The proposed modification
   introduces an unapproved and unsafe deviation from the subdivision’s established
   pattern, potentially affecting shared stormwater performance. As such, the proposal is
   not compatible with the subdivision’s design character or existing residential access
   patterns.

   The application is incompatible with the surrounding residential development pattern
   and the subdivision’s recorded design intent.

   Applicant Response: The proposed parking is compatible with the surrounding area.
   The drainage of the Silver Leaf subdivision is not negatively impacted by the redesigned
   parking space with a culvert. The residential access patterns of the Silver Leaf subdivision
   for all of the units, other than Unit 4, have safe and easy pedestrian pathways. Unit 4 was
   uniquely burdened by the relocation of the guest parking from the original Silver Leaf site
   plans and Jacks any reasonable means of pedestrian access to Unit 4. Unit 4's access is
   compromised by substantial cobble rock which creates an unstable and hazardous
   walking surface that has been further undermined by the erection of a fence that blocks
   a pathway free of cobbles. The current pedestrian pathway to Unit 4 from the existing
   guest parking is virtually unusable and is certainly hazardous. The proposed parking
   space is not intended to solely serve Unit 4, rather the parking space will be available for
   use by other Silver Leaf owners and guests. The proposed parking will be similar in
   appearance to other parking in the Silver Leaf development.

   New Findings: The new plans address the drainage issues. The design did originally
   provide a set of two driveways near the proposed site, which were removed on the next
   iteration of the plan. The new designs do not specify the type of gravel to be used, so the
   character of the driveway cannot be ascertained in relation to the development.

(3) The desirability and need for the proposed use.

   While the applicant’s intent to add off-street parking is understandable, the subdivision
   was approved and constructed with designated parking and access consistent with Town
   standards. The proposed additional driveway does not represent a public necessity and
   may undermine the approved subdivision drainage system. Therefore, the desirability




                                                                                       Page 90 of 614
                     TOWN OF PAONIA
                     PLANNING COMMISSION & COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT
                     STAFF REPORT

   and need are outweighed by the potential negative impacts on shared infrastructure and
   public safety.

   The proposed additional parking does not represent a public necessity and may harm
   established infrastructure.

   Applicant Response: When Silver Leaf was originally designed the guest parking area
   on the east side of the development off Clark Avenue was designed in a location that did
   provide an easy direct pedestrian pathway to Unit 4 as well as other units. However, that
   guest parking area was not built in accordance with the original plans as confirmed by
   staff's note that the as-built plans for Silver Leaf showed the deviation of the location of
   the existing guest parking area. As a result of the change in the guest parking location,
   Unit 4 was uniquely and singularly isolated from close access to parked vehicles. Access
   to Unit 4 is over cobble and gravel paths that are difficult to on walk at times, and difficult,
   if not impossible, for a handicapped person to traverse.

   Additionally, the existing guest parking off Clark Ave does not have a clear and easy access
   path to Unit 4. In fact, a fence has been erected blocking access to Unit 4 from the current
   guest parking area off of Clark Avenue. The existing configuration of guest parking along
   Clark Avenue prohibits handicap access to Unit 4 in a community that was designed for
   seniors and those aging in place. The proposed parking space will provide handicap
   access directly to Unit 4.

   The proposed parking is necessary to provide adequate and safe pedestrian access to Unit
   4, particularly given the obstructions that have been constructed from the guest parking
   area that have created a hazardous impassable pedestrian path. The proposed parking
   provides a safe and ADA accessible pathway to Unit 4. All other units that are provided
   access from the existing guest parking have clear and safe pedestrian pathways. There is
   no harm to established infrastructure. The proposed parking space will not obstruct
   stormwater flow and detention as was originally proposed for Silver Leaf as a culvert will
   be installed under the parking space.

   New Findings: The new design does address the reported problems with public
   infrastructure. The new design, however, is not ADA compliant. Per the ADA Standards
   for Accessible Design §403.3, “The running slope of walking surfaces shall not be steeper
   than 1:20 [5%]. The cross slope of walking surfaces shall not be steeper than 1:48
   [2.08%].” Similarly, §502.2-4 stipulates the minimum width of an accessible space must
   be 96 inches with a 60 inch aisle (13 feet total). The downhill and cross slopes at the end
   of the driveway are 7.5%, and the walkway is listed with a maximum slope of 8%. This
   spot does not provide ADA access nor ADA parking.

(4) The potential for adverse environmental influences that might result from the proposed use.




                                                                                           Page 91 of 614
                    TOWN OF PAONIA
                    PLANNING COMMISSION & COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT
                    STAFF REPORT

   The proposed driveway is located directly within an engineered bioswale designed to
   convey runoff from Clark Avenue. Altering this drainage feature by filling or paving could
   reduce stormwater flow capacity, create ponding, and redirect runoff toward adjacent
   properties. This would constitute an adverse environmental influence contrary to Section
   3.1 of the Silverleaf Development Agreement requiring preservation of common drainage
   improvements.

   The project presents clear adverse environmental impacts by obstructing stormwater
   infrastructure.

   Applicant Response: The proposed parking has been redesigned, from the original
   submission, to specifically include a culvert to ensure that the drainage is not altered. The
   addition of the culvert actually improves the drainage within this specific area.
   Stormwater is not obstructed, rather it is now channeled through the culvert.

   The current stormwater detention facility is below surface and therefore is intended to
   store water and then release it on the downstream side to the next subsurface detention
   facility. The proposed parking space does not obstruct this function, and by adding a
   culvert will enhance the conveyance of any stormwater flowing on the surface.

   New Findings: The drainage issues have been addressed with the new plans. No evidence
   for “enhance[d]” stormwater conveyance is present, however.

(5) Compatibility of the proposed use and site plan with the policies and guidelines of the
    Comprehensive Plan.

   The 2025 Paonia Comprehensive Plan establishes a clear policy framework under its
   Infrastructure Element that prioritizes dependable, safe, and sustainable infrastructure
   systems. The Plan envisions infrastructure that is proactive, environmentally
   responsible, and protective of community assets and neighboring properties. Several
   adopted goals and policies directly relate to the proposed site modification:

              •   INFRA Goal 2 calls for the Town to “plan, budget, operate, and maintain
                  stormwater, water treatment, and green infrastructure that support quality
                  public services in health, water availability, and safety.”

              •   INFRA Goal 11 seeks to “protect the community from adverse flooding and
                  pollution impacts of runoff with efficient and progressive stormwater
                  management practices.”

              •   Stormwater Policy 2 directs the Town to “codify and create standards for
                  stormwater mitigation requirements for new development and ensure that
                  drainage system design and construction are kept current,” while




                                                                                        Page 92 of 614
                             TOWN OF PAONIA
                             PLANNING COMMISSION & COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT
                             STAFF REPORT

                           Stormwater Policy 3 requires that “new development preserve natural
                           drainage courses and, where necessary, be supported by an engineered
                           drainage system.”
            Applicant Response: The proposed design is compatible with all policies and guidelines
            of the Town's comprehensive plan. It preserves and enhances the natural and engineered
            infrastructure that currently exists. The proposed engineered design is above the current
            grade and bioswale, which is completely below the surface. Water was intended to flow
            into the bioswale and exit on the downstream end of the swale to enter into the next one
            downstream. The proposed parking space does not inhibit this function. Moreover, the
            addition of a culvert to the design will allow water that would otherwise slowly flow
            under the parking space in the bioswale to flow directly down the swale as originally
            intended.

            New Findings: Stormwater management issues have been addressed in the new space.
            No evidence of improved stormwater management is present.

(b) Lights and signs shall be located in a manner that will not be distracting to adjoining properties or
    passing motorists.

    No additional lighting or signage is proposed as part of this application. Therefore, this criterion
    is not applicable.

(c) Landscaping shall be provided in areas near public right-of-way and located with consideration for
    energy conservation. An acceptable plan must be provided for the maintenance of the required
    landscaped areas.

    The driveway encroaches into an area designated for stormwater management rather than
    landscaped open space. Disturbing this feature would eliminate vegetation critical to infiltration
    and erosion control. Restoration or replacement of this landscaping would be necessary if any
    work were approved.

    Applicant Response: No vegetation critical to the infiltration and erosion control will be
    eliminated. The area along Clark A venue adjacent to Silver Leaf has been landscaped, including
    substantial additional landscaping installed by the owner of Unit 4 in the fall of 2024 and summer
    of 2025. The proposed parking will not eliminate or disturb those landscaped areas, particularly
    the areas landscape by and at the expense of the owner of Unit 4.

    New Findings: Some of the issues have been addressed with the culvert. Restoration of disturbed
    landscaping would still be necessary if any work were approved.

(d) Control of storm drainage shall be provided so as to not damage adjoining properties. The plan must
    be approved by the Town Engineer.




                                                                                                 Page 93 of 614
                             TOWN OF PAONIA
                             PLANNING COMMISSION & COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT
                             STAFF REPORT

   Control of storm drainage is a critical deficiency in this application. The proposed location within
   the bioswale would obstruct designed flow paths and potentially damage adjoining properties.
   Any approval would require an engineered drainage plan reviewed and certified by a Colorado-
   licensed engineer and approved by the Town Administrator or designee. Without such
   documentation, the plan fails to satisfy this standard.

   The design does not include an engineered culvert or certified drainage plan to maintain the
   bioswale’s function. Any alteration of a subdivision drainage facility requires professional
   engineering review and Town approval.

   Without such a design, the project fails to meet the performance standard for drainage control
   under §16-4-50(d) and the intent of Article 6.

   Applicant Response: The currently proposed engineered parking does not obstruct or divert
   storm drainage resulting in damage to adjacent properties. The bioswale functions will be
   maintained. Storm water within this area will be further directed through the proposed culvert
   and will assist in directing storm water in its intended course.

   New Findings: This issue has been adequately addressed

(e) Site design and building plans shall include provisions for the needs of handicapped individuals as
    required by the Building Code or other ordinances of the Town.

   Not applicable; the proposal concerns vehicular access to a private unit and does not include
   pedestrian or ADA-accessible design elements.

   Applicant Response: The proposed design includes an ADA accessible ramp from the driveway
   to grade and into the yard of Unit 4.

   New Findings: Neither the parking spot nor the flagstone path are ADA compliant.

(f) Approved landscaping or solid fencing capable of screening adjacent properties shall be provided
    where commercial uses abut residential uses.

   Not applicable; the use is residential in character and does not abut a commercial zone.

(g) Commercial and Industrial uses shall conform to the following performance standards:


    Not applicable; the subject property is zoned R-2 (Medium Density Residential) and does not
    involve commercial or industrial activity.
Additional notes: The plan does not address the requested Concrete Edge Treatment (A reinforced
concrete edge pan minimum 6 inches wide and 8 inches deep where the driveway meets the road).




                                                                                               Page 94 of 614
                            TOWN OF PAONIA
                            PLANNING COMMISSION & COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT
                            STAFF REPORT

Nor does it address the request for post-construction certification, the request for a certified
engineer’s letter confirming that the bioswale and roadway edge have been restored to design
standards.
Additionally, due to the placement of the bioswale and the height of the driveway, erosion control
measures should be considered to protect the surrounding drainage.

Summary of Findings:
Based on the evidence and documentation reviewed, the proposed driveway and parking space at
1200 3rd Street, Unit 4:

   •   Provides a culvert to address the major drainage and bioswale issues.
   •   Follows most of the PMC and DCRDCS building standards and recommendations. Variances
       that should be addressed are: a minimum of 6” and ideally 12” of Class 6 beneath the
       driveway’s gravel and above the compacted subgrade, use of corrugated, galvanized metal
       with metal end sections for the culvert, clear notes that the pipe extends a minimum of 12”
       beyond the embankment/shoulder, adding 6” of class 6 bedding over the culvert, specific
       information about the driveway’s gravel (ideally in comparison to the subdivision’s current
       parking spaces), and notes on the restoration of disturbed vegetation.
   •   Does not provide any ADA features.
   •   Conflicts with the Silverleaf Development Agreement (2016) and most recent available
       plans.

Staff Analysis:

The applicant’s revised proposal solves the largest issues presented at the previous meeting,
including addressing the stormwater drainage and driveway design. The driveway itself is positioned
in line with PMC and follows most applicable standards, with the exceptions outlined above.

The project is an addition to an existing planned unit development with an overarching design that
was revised several times in tandem with the Town. Later changes to this plan should be considered
with the current HOA’s opinions, the design and intent of the newest available plan set, and the
character of the development in mind.

Staff Recommendation
Staff recommends Conditional Approval of SRV 2025-01 subject to the following minimum
requirements:

   1. Additional Approvals: The Silver Leaf HOA provides their approval for the project in written
      form.
   2. Culvert Design Modifications: Notes on the engineered plan showing the use of corrugated,
      galvanized metal with metal end sections for the culvert, demonstrating the pipe extends a




                                                                                            Page 95 of 614
                          TOWN OF PAONIA
                          PLANNING COMMISSION & COMMUNITY DEVELOPMENT
                          STAFF REPORT

     minimum of 12” beyond the embankment/shoulder, and adding a minimum 6” of class 6
     aggregate base course material over the culvert.
  3. Driveway Design Modifications: The addition of a minimum of 6” and ideally 12” of Class 6
     base course beneath the driveway’s gravel and above the compacted subgrade and
     information about the type of gravel composing the top of the driveway.
  4. Concrete Edge Treatment or Driveway Apron: The installation of a reinforced concrete
     edge pan (minimum 6 inches wide and 8 inches deep) OR a 3” deep and 2’ long asphalt apron
     at the street interface.
  5. Post-Construction Certification: The provision of a certified engineer’s letter confirming
     that the bioswale, vegetation, and roadway edge have been restored to design standards.

Potential Motions:

  1) I move to recommend conditional approval of SRV 2025-01, subject to the conditions outlined
     by staff, finding that the application can meet the intent of Town standards with appropriate
     design modifications.

  2) I move to recommend denial of SRV 2025-01 as the proposed driveway conflicts with the
     Silverleaf Development Agreement and adopted roadway standards, and does not serve the
     public health, safety, and welfare.

From: Michael Cook
Sent: Tuesday, March 31, 2026 10:24 AM
To: Stefen Wynn <StefenW@townofpaonia.com>
Cc: Daoine B <DaoineB@townofpaonia.com>;                               Jeff Ruppert
Subject: Supplement to Materials Submitted on March 19, 2026, in Support of Proposed Additional Guest Parking Space, SVR
2025-01, 1200 3rd Street, Unit 4


CAUTION: This email originated from outside your organization. Exercise caution when opening attachments or
clicking links, especially from unknown senders.


Mr. Wynn,

In preparing for the April 6, 2026 Planning Commission hearing on this matter, some new information of
significance has come to my attention. I believe this new information is important to share with Town Staff and
the Planning Commission prior to the upcoming hearing. Accordingly, please include this email and its
attachments with the Planning Commission Packet for the April 6, 2026 hearing.

1. The Development Agreement between the Town and Silver Leaf mandates that Silver Leaf be
constructed in compliance with ADA requirements.
                                                                 1
                                                                                                            Page 110 of 614
Silver Leaf was processed and approved by the Town as a Special Review Application. Accordingly, pursuant to
the terms of the Development Agreement and Municipal Code Section 14-4-60, Silver Leaf was required to be
built in strict compliance with "the approved application, the site plan and any written proposals submitted
therewith . . ."

The approved Silver Leaf application contains numerous representations that the development would be
designed for senior citizens, with homes universally designed to be wheelchair accessible and providing
housing that would be 'barrier free'. (See attached excerpts from the Development Agreement and approved
application.)

Even more specifically, the application contains the following question by the Town and answer by the
developer:

       i. Detail the Safety and Disabled Access accommodation?

       All new structures will be ADA accessible and will be connected by 4' wide pathways with appropriately
       designed wheelchair accessible ramps where changes in grade require it. (emphasis added)

Additionally, the design and construction notes, which are part of the Development Agreement and approved
Special Review Application, make periodic references to ADA compliance requirements for the development of
Silver Leaf.

The Silver Leaf Special Review Application, incorporated into the terms and conditions of the Silver Leaf
Development Agreement, specifically represented that Silver Leaf would be developed in compliance with ADA
requirements. Accordingly, the Silver Leaf Development Agreement and the Town of Paonia's Municipal Code
mandate that Silver Leaf comply with ADA requirements. As admitted by Silver Leaf’s Board of Directors, Silver
Leaf is not ADA compliant. Perhaps the most glaring failure is the total lack of anything even close to ADA
compliant access to Unit 4. The proposed additional guest parking space will rectify that glaring and
unlawful failure.

If this matter is not amicably resolved, Silver Leaf may be obligated to correct that failure at its expense.

2. The location of the guest parking on the west side of Silver Leaf as shown on the site plans that
are part of the Development Agreement would have been ADA compliant.

The Silver Leaf site plans that are part of the Development Agreement locate the guest parking on the west side
of Silver Leaf in a location that would have been ADA compliant. This site plan location is the same location
being requested for the additional parking space. (See attached site plans.)

When the guest parking on the west side was moved during construction to its current location, no amendment
to the Development Agreement or site plan was ever processed, as required by the Municipal
Code. Accordingly, the current guest parking location is unlawful. While one might consider it a technical
violation that might now be corrected with an amendment, the significance of the original location for guest
parking is that the original location would have prevented Unit 4 from being isolated. Undoubtedly, the
designers of Silver Leaf recognized that a parking space located in the originally specified location, now being
requested, was necessary for ADA accessibility compliance.

3. The Silver Leaf Bylaws mandate that it comply with all Federal and State laws regarding housing for
older persons.

                                                          2
                                                                                                   Page 111 of 614
In September 2023, Silver Leaf amended its Bylaws to officially memorialize in its governing documents that
Silver Leaf is an age restricted community consistent with the Silver Leaf Special Review Application and the
representations contained therein. The amended Bylaws established Silver Leaf as an age-restricted,
independent living community for adults primarily 55 years of age or older. Becoming an age restricted
community effectively allowed Silver Leaf to discriminate against younger adults and peop le with small children
by mandating that 80% of the residents had to be over fifty-five and not allowing any residents younger than 30
years of age. The 2023 Bylaw amendment, a copy which is attached, states:

       "From today forward, our association and community shall comply with all applicable Federal and
       State laws regarding housing for older persons."

Those Federal and State laws include, but are not necessarily limited to, the Americans with Disabilities Act
(ADA), the Colorado and Federal Fair Housing Acts, and the Colorado Common Interest Ownership Act
(CCIOA). The current isolation and lack of compliant access to Unit 4 violates all of those acts, as well as the
Paonia Municipal Code, as described above.

4. The Colorado Common Interest Ownership Act (CCIOA), which is the overriding statutory law in
Colorado governing HOA's, specifically prohibits Silver Leaf from refusing to allow the requested
reasonable parking space modification.

Pursuant to your request, I asked the Silver Leaf HOA to provide written approval of my proposal. (See attached
March 19, 2026 email to HOA Board of Directors.) To date, the HOA has not done so, indicating it has questions
regarding liability and maintenance for the Town. My response has been that I am reasonably certain the Town
will require the HOA to be responsible for both, in the same way that the HOA has always been responsible for
all current driveways and guest and assigned parking in Silver Leaf. I have no idea if the requested approval
from the HOA will be forthcoming. If it is not, the following statutory provision from the CCIOA is applicable:

       C.R.S. Section 38-33.3-106.5. Prohibitions contrary to public policy . . . . (1) Notwithstanding any
       provisions in the declarations, bylaws, or rules and regulations of the association to the contrary, an
       association shall not prohibit any of the following: . . . . (g) Reasonable modifications to a unit or to
       common elements as necessary to afford a person with disabilities full use and enjoyment of the unit in
       accordance with the federal "Fair Housing Act of 1968", 42 U.S.C. sec. 3604 (f)(3)(A).

Conclusion

For all of the forgoing reasons, as well as for the reasons previously stated in the materials submitted in support
of my proposal, the current situation involving the isolation and lack of required access to Unit 4 violates
Paonia's Municipal Code, Federal and State laws and Silver Leaf’s own Bylaws. If allowed, the requested
additional parking space will alleviate the current unlawful situation regarding access to Unit 4.

Michael Cook

From: Michael Cook

Subject: Revised Project Description for Special Use Review Application of Michael Cook for Additional Guest Parking Space, SVR
2025-01 1200 3rd Street, Unit 4


CAUTION: This email originated from outside your organization. Exercise caution when opening attachments or
clicking links, especially from unknown senders.


Mr. Wynn,

The purpose of this email is to confirm that Jeff Ruppert P.E. will be delivering to the Town of Paonia a Revised
Project Description for my Special Use Review Application, SVR 2025-01, which is currently scheduled for
public hearing before the Planning Commission on April 6, 2026. A detailed description of the revised project is
contained in the report letter and design details prepared by Mr. Ruppert, which you will receive by hand delivery
today. A signed copy of this email with hard copies of Mr. Ruppert's report letter, design details on 11" x 17"
paper and a copy of a page from the 2016 site plan for the project will be hand delivered to you. (A similar site
plan showing the same originally planed guest parking location is part of the Town's Development Agreement
with Silver Leaf.)

As you know, the staff report prepared for the November 3, 2025 Planning Commission hearing recommended
denial of the application as then submitted. The staff report, however, described an alternative path for
possible approval which would involve:

      Preparing an engineered drainage and grading plan stamped by a Colorado-licensed professional
       engineer;
      Designing and sizing a culvert sufficient to maintain the bioswale's drainage function;
      Realigning the driveway to be perpendicular to Clark Avenue with safe approach grades;
      Including durable surfacing and concrete edge treatment specifications.

After receiving that staff report, Mr. Ruppert was retained to determine whether a parking space could be
designed that would be compliant with all applicable state and local parking related regulations. After a site
survey was performed by Wilmore Company, Mr. Ruppert concluded that could be done. We believe his report
letter and design for the additional parking space achieves that goal.

As you may know, Mr. Ruppert was the engineer who prepared the civil engineering design for the Silver Leaf
development, including the design for the control of storm drainage that the staff considered a critical
deficiency in my original project description. Accordingly, as the design engineer for the original project, he is
uniquely qualified to prepare the engineered design and drainage plan for this project.

While Mr. Ruppert's report and design describe the engineering details relating to the project, I want to also
briefly provide some additional reasons why I believe the revised project is appropriate and necessary.

      The Silver Leaf development is designated as a senior living community, intended to be appropriate and
       convenient for elderly residents. A community in which older people can move and navigate easily and
       safely. Unit 4 is the only unit that does not have reasonably close parking access for loading and
       unloading, and for accessing the unit. Even more significantly, the available access to Unit 4 is not
       practically or technically compliant with access requirements for disabled individuals. Every other unit
                                                               3
                                                                                                           Page 119 of 614
       in Silver Leaf has available access which at least approaches ADA compliance. The requested parking
       space will provide convenient, safe and ADA compliant access to Unit 4, consistent with the needs and
       requirements for a senior living community.

      The original design documents for Silver Leaf, which are incorporated into the Development Agreement
       with the Town of Paonia, show a parking space located almost exactly where the requested parking
       space will be located. (See the site plan from Mr. Ruppert's files which shows the location of the
       originally planned parking space.) The staff report states that the as-built drawings for the project
       indicate the originally planned parking spot was relocated for ease of construction. The fact that the
       original design located a parking space exactly where the requested space will be located underscores
       and demonstrates that parking in the requested location is appropriate and consistent with the design
       intent for the Silver Leaf development.

      The requested additional guest parking space may relieve and minimize some of the currently
       unauthorized parking that occurs along Clark Street during certain times of the week, will create a
       conveniently located additional guest parking space for the Silver Leaf community and will provide an
       additional EV charging station at Silver Leaf for guests and residents.

For these reasons, as well as those stated in Mr. Ruppert's report, I request and hope the Town Staff and
Planning Commission will approve the application for this revised project.

Best Regards,


Michael Cook

                                        Planning Commission Minutes

RECORD OF PROCEEDINGS

Commissioner Bachran calls the meeting to order at 6:00 PM.

A)    Roll Call
      Present:
      Commissioner Bachran
      Commissioner Smith
      Commissioner Stelter
      Commissioner Wynn

      Absent:
      Vice-Chair Howe
      Commissioner Hunter

B)    Approval of Agenda
      Commissioner Wynn makes a motion to remove the Appointment of Officers (item “D”) from
      the agenda due to not all members being present. Seconded by Commissioner Stelter.

      The motion carries unanimously.

       Commissioner Wynn makes a motion to remove Actions & Presentations (item “F”) from the
       agenda as there are none scheduled. Seconded by Commissioner Stelter.

       The motion carries unanimously.

       Commissioner Stelter makes a motion to approve the agenda as amended. Seconded by
       Commissioner Smith.

       The motion carries unanimously.

C)     Approval of Minutes

       1) October 6, 2025, Planning Commission Meeting Minutes
          Commissioner Stelter makes a motion to approve the October 6, 2025, minutes.
          Seconded by Commissioner Wynn.

          The motion carries unanimously.

D)     Appointment of Officers
       Item removed during approval of agenda.
                                                            4      I A . Ir   ;.. H
E)     Public Hearing

       2) Consideration of a Special Use Review to add an additional guest parking spot, west of
       Unit #4 in the Silver Leaf Co-Housing Development at the address of 1200 3rd St, Unit 4
       Paonia, CO, 81428 from Michael Cook.

       Commissioner Bachran opens the public hearing at 6:02 PM.

       Town Administrator Wynn presents the staff report, which reported that the proposed
       driveway and parking space at 1200 Third Street, Unit 4, violated multiple sections of the
       Town Code, Delta County roadway standards, and the Silver Leaf Development
       Agreement by encroaching on an engineered bioswale and creating drainage and safety
       risks. He stated the project conflicted with the Town’s 2025 Comprehensive Plan goals
       for safe, sustainable infrastructure and recommended denial as submitted, with possible
       conditional approval only if redesigned with engineered drainage, proper alignment, and
       compliance with all stormwater and safety requirements.

November 3, 2025, Planning Commission Meeting Minutes                                               1



                                                                                           Page 137 of 614
       Applicant Michael Cook addressed the Commission, acknowledging that the original
       design did not comply with Town requirements. He apologized and stated he had
       retained engineer Jeff Ruppert, of Odisea Engineering, who designed the original civil
       plans to develop a compliant design per the conditional path outlined by staff. Mr. Cook
       requested the opportunity to revise the proposal rather than proceed with denial.

       Public Comment:

       R. Hunter, adjacent neighbor, supported the staffs findings and suggested expanding the
       existing guest parking area instead of creating a new space.

       No objections or protests were submitted.

       Commission Discussion Topics Included: underground utility conflicts, existing parking
       expansion, existing EV charger location, original intentions of maintaining limited traffic
       & parking, and ensuring that future engineering plans fully address drainage, grading,
       safety, and code compliance before reconsideration.

       Commissioner Bachran closes the public hearing at 6:46 PM.

       Commissioner Stelter moves to table this subject until the March 2, 2026, Planning
       Commission meeting. Seconded by Commissioner Wynn

       Discussion: Question for Clerk Vetter, if re-noticing/re-publishing is required when the
       time comes.

       The motion carries unanimously.

F)     Actions & Presentations

       Item removed during approval of agenda.

G)     Adjournment

       Commissioner Stelter motions to adjourn. Seconded by Commissioner Wynn.

       The motion carries unanimously.

       Commissioner Bachran adjourns the meeting at 6:49 PM.
