                                              C.R.S. 31-4-304


                                                Copy Citation


   Statutes current through Chapter 36 of the 2026 Regular Session, effective as of April 6, 2026. The text
    of this section is not final. It will not be final until compared to, and updated from, the text provided by

                         the Colorado Office of Legislative Legal Services later this year.

   Colorado Revised Statutes Annotated            Title 31. Government - Municipal (§§ 31-1-101 — 31-

   35-712)      Corporate Class - Organization and Territory (Arts. 1 — 4)            Article 4.

   Organizational Structure and Officers (Pts. 1 — 5)           Part 3. Organizational Structure and

   Officers of Statutory Towns (§§ 31-4-301 — 31-4-307)



   31-4-304. Appointment of officers - compensation.
   The board of trustees shall appoint a clerk, treasurer, and town attorney, or shall provide by ordinance

   for the election of such officers, and may appoint such other officers, including a town administrator, as it

   deems necessary for the good government of the corporation, and it shall prescribe by ordinance their

   duties when the same are not defined by law and the compensation or fees they are entitled to receive

   for their services. The board of trustees may require officers to take an oath or affirmation in accordance

   with section 24-12-101. The election of officers shall be at the regular election, and no appointment of

   any officer shall continue beyond thirty days after compliance with section 31-4-401 by the members of

   the succeeding board of trustees.




   History



   Source: L. 75:Entire title R&RE, p. 1034, § 1, effective July 1. L. 83:Entire section amended, p. 1258, §

   12, effective July 1. L. 91:Entire section amended, p. 745, § 14, effective April 4. L. 2018:Entire section

   amended,(HB 18-1138), ch. 88, p. 698, § 29, effective August 8; entire section amended,(HB 18-1140),

   ch. 41, p. 464, § 7, effective August 8.

ANNOTATION

Law reviews.

For note, “Rights of a Holdover Trustee Under Colorado Law of 1929”, see 2 Rocky Mt. L. Rev. 254
(1930).

Annotator’s note.

Since § 31-4-304 is similar to former § 31-3-304 prior to the 1975 repeal and reenactment of this
title, and laws antecedent thereto, a relevant case construing a prior provision has been included in
the annotations to this section.

Duties of town attorney left to board.

There is no statute defining the duties of town attorneys of incorporated towns, but under the
provisions of this section, it is left to the board of trustees of such a municipality to fix and
determine the character of services to be rendered and compensation to be paid to its attorneys.
Kinzie v. Haxtun, 97 Colo. 456, 50 P.2d 545 (1935).

Decisions by town attorney and manager are discretionary as a matter of law and entitled

to official immunity




                                                                                              Page 552 of 614
        if such decisions are of a judgmental, planning, or policy nature and the officials are acting in their
        official capacities and not outside the scope of their offices. Troxel v. Town of Basalt, 682 P.2d 501
        (Colo. App. 1984).




        Research References & Practice Aids
        Cross references:

        (1) For the legislative declaration in HB 18-1138, see section 1 of chapter 88, Session Laws of
        Colorado 2018.

        (2) For the legislative declaration in HB 18-1140, see section 1 of chapter 41, Session Laws of
        Colorado 2018.

                                  RESOLUTION 26-06
A RESOLUTION OF THE BOARD OF TRUSTEES OF THE TOWN OF PAONIA
DESIGNATING AUTHORIZED SIGNATURES ON APPLICATIONS OF NEW OR CHANGES
TO BANK AND CREDIT ACCOUNTS
WHEREAS, the Town Board believes it would be appropriate and in accordance with proper
auditing, bookkeeping, and accounting standards for the purpose of maintaining best
practices for municipal financial external controls to require a combination of staff and
elected official signatures on all applications to set up new or make changes to bank and
credit accounts.
WHEREAS, on April 28, 2026, Mayor Pro Tem Rick Stelter finished his term on the board of
Trustees; and
WHEREAS, on April 28, 2026 Trustee _____________ was appointed Mayor Pro-Tem for the
Paonia Board of Trustees.
NOW THEREFORE, BE IT RESOLVED by the Board of Trustees of the Town of Paonia,
Colorado, that:
Section 1. All applications for bank accounts or credit accounts shall require the following
signatures:
1. Stefen Wynn (Town Administrator) or Samira Vetter (Town Clerk);
AND
2. Paige Smith (Mayor) or ______________________ (Mayor/Pro-Tem)
OR
3. Walter Czech (Finance Committee member)
Section 2. All such setting up new accounts or changes to accounts, shall henceforth require
a combination of one of the persons listed in paragraph #1 and one of the persons listed in
paragraph #2 or #3 above.
THIS RESOLUTION WAS READ, PASSED AND ADOPTED by the Board of Trustees of the
Town of Paonia on this 28th day of April 2026.




Attest:


Samira M. Vetter, Town Clerk                            Paige Smith, Mayor

AGENDA ITEM:      Street name sign replacement proposal



SUBMITTED BY:     Rodney Byrge


DATE:
                  03/23/2026

BACKGROUND:       There are many street name signs in the Town of Paonia that are
                  faded/damaged and in need of replacement. Currently, the signs consist of
                  various designs, layout, color and do not meet MUTCD (Manual on Uniform
                  Traffic Control Devices) regulations.




BUDGET:



                  Paonia Public Works would like to have a uniform street name sign that
RECOMMENDATION:   complies with MUTCD standards and remove prefixes and suffixes from the
                  signs. Public Works recommends moving forward with Brimar. They were
                  excellent to work with.

                  analysis, and vendor-provided information. The attached materials collectively

                  operationally, and how it aligns with applicable legal standards. I believe the review
                  also identifies an opportunity for the Board to consider whether a more formal,
                  Town-wide policy framework governing use, oversight, and administration of the
                  system would be appropriate moving forward.

                                           MEMORANDUM

TO:          Mayor & Board of Trustees
FROM:        Stefen Wynn, Town Administrator & Treasurer
DATE:        4/24/2026
SUBJECT: Executive Summary – Verkada Camera System Review, Use, and Policy Considerations




This memorandum is intended to address the questions raised regarding the Town’s Verkada camera
system by organizing them into key subject areas, providing a summary response to each, and identifying
where each question is addressed in greater detail within the supporting attachments. The questions
summarized below came from the compiled “Camera Discussion” document and re�lect operational, legal,
�inancial, and policy considerations.


1. DATA ACCESS, STORAGE AND CONTROL

Questions Considered:
      •   Does Verkada have access to the 30-day footage stored on its cloud?
      •   If the company is sold, what happens to the stored data and information?
      •   Is footage still accessible after the 30-day retention period?
      •   Is data stored on third party servers accessible by others, and can this be veri�ied?
      •   Where and how is data encrypted?
      •   What has Verkada’s response been after the cyber-security attack on their customers and what
          has been done since the settlement with the FTC?

Summary of Responses:

In my professional opinion, based on currently available information and vendor representations, the Town
retains ownership and control of its data. Footage is encrypted in transit and at rest, and it is my
understanding that neither Verkada nor third-party providers access that data without authorization. Video
is retained for approximately 30 days unless archived, after which it is no longer accessible.

Which Attachments Give Detailed Answers:

      •   Camera Discussion (Data Access section)
      •   Verkada Security and Privacy Resources




                                                                                                Page 573 of 614
2. FACIAL RECOGNITION AND ADVANCED TECHNOLOGY

Questions Considered:

   •   Can facial recognition be turned on remotely, even if currently disabled?
   •   Can facial recognition be applied retroactively to stored footage?
   •   If disabled locally, could it still be enabled or used by others in the future?
   •   Are there safeguards preventing data sharing with federal agencies such as DHS or ICE without a
       federal warrant?

Summary of Responses:

Based on the information available, and from entering the system to verify, the system includes features
that are currently disabled and not in use. Facial recognition and similar features are currently disabled and
require intentional action by a user with administrative permissions to activate. It is my understanding from
discussing with Verkada representatives that these features cannot be enabled remotely by the vendor and
that any changes would be logged. I also understand that the system does not connect to external identity
databases and that any data sharing would require action by the Town or an appropriate legal process.

Which Attachments Give Detailed Answers:

   •   Camera Discussion
   •   Verkada Security and Privacy Resources



3. LEGAL COMPLIANCE AND PRIVACY PROTECTIONS

Questions Considered:

   •   Are live cameras accessible in a manner that complies with Colorado Law, including warrant
       requirements?
   •   Does audio recording violate federal laws such as the Electronic Communications Privacy Act or
       wiretap statutes?
   •   How does single party consent law in Colorado apply to these recordings?
   •   What is the legal standing of the end user agreement and associated terms?

Summary of Response:

Based on the Town Attorney’s review, I believe the current use of cameras is consistent with Colorado law,
particularly as it relates to recording in areas where there is no reasonable expectation of privacy. It is my
understanding that warrants are not required for standard public security surveillance systems and that
audio recording may be permissible under Colorado’s one-party consent framework. The applicability of
federal law has also been addressed, with the conclusion that typical public security surveillance systems
of this type are not governed by the Electronic Communications Privacy Act.

Which Attachments Give Detailed Answers:

   •   Re_Legal Questions RE_Verkada Cameras
   •   Camera Discussion (Legal Section)




                                                                                                  Page 574 of 614
4. USE, OVERSIGHT, AND GOVERNANCE

Questions Considered:

   •   What quali�ies as an “incident” for purposes of recording or archiving footage?
   •   Who is accessing the footage and for what purposes?
   •   How are interactions documented, and can recordings be edited, corrected, or challenged?
   •   What are the other cameras referenced in the CORA request?
   •   What guidelines govern staff use of the system?

Summary of Responses:

In my professional opinion, current practices re�lect a controlled and purpose-driven approach to system
use. The Police Department operates under a formal policy with de�ined access controls, while Public Works
and administrative staff appear to use the system primarily in response to speci�ic incidents or operational
needs. That said, I believe the questions raised highlight an opportunity to develop a more comprehensive,
Town-wide policy to ensure consistency in governance and oversight.

Which Attachments Give Detailed Answers:

   •   PD Camera System Staff Report 2026
   •   Public Works Verkada camera uses
   •   Clerk Verkada Camera Use
   •   Camera Discussion



5. NECESSITY, PURPOSE AND SCOPE OF THE SYSTEM

Questions Considered:

   •   Is this level of surveillance necessary for a small town like Paonia?
   •   Has the system been used for intended purposes such as water system monitoring?
   •   Are cameras in locations such as parks and public spaces justi�ied?
   •   Could alternative approaches (eg. Community vigilance or staf�ing) achieve the same goals?

Summary of Response:

I believe the system was implemented in response to speci�ic operational challenges, including vandalism,
theft, and unauthorized access to facilities. Based on the information provided, cameras are being used to
monitor infrastructure and support operational decision-making. In my professional opinion, alternatives
such as increased staf�ing or reliance on community monitoring would likely be less effective and more
resource-intensive.

Which Attachments Give Detailed Answers:

   •   Public Works Verkada camera uses
   •   PD Camera System Staff Report 2026
   •   Camera Discussion




                                                                                                Page 575 of 614
6. FINANCIAL JUSTIFICATION

Questions Considered:

   •   Is the expense of the camera system justi�ied relative to its bene�its?
   •   Was the system funded by grants or taxpayer dollars?
   •   Could a less expensive alternative have been considered?

Summary of Responses:

In my professional opinion, the system appears to represent a cost-effective approach to protecting Town
assets and infrastructure. It is my understanding that funding was derived from multiple sources, including
enterprise funds and capital allocations, rather than solely from general tax revenues. No grants were
received speci�ically for procurement of the camera system.

Which Attachments Give Detailed Answers:

   •   Camera Discussion (Financial Section)
   •   Verkada Security and Privacy Resources



7. AUDIO RECORDING AND SURVEILLANCE SCOPE

Questions Considered:

   •   Do the cameras record audio, and are individuals being monitored or “listened to” in Town facilities?
   •   Are recordings continuous or only motion activated?

Summary of Responses:

Based on the information provided, I understand that certain cameras have audio capability, though it must
be intentionally enabled and is not universally active. Only cameras equipped with audio capability include
a toggle function on the system dashboard. Video is continuously recording with motion �lagging to assist
in review. I believe the use of audio is limited and subject to applicable legal standards as described by the
Town Attorney. To the extent that prior descriptions have simpli�ied how these features operate, I believe
this additional context helps clarify the technical distinction while remaining consistent with the system’s
intended uses.

Which Attachments Give Detailed Answers:

   •   Re_Legal Questions RE_Verkada Cameras
   •   Verkada Security and Privacy Resources
   •   Camera Discussion (Audio Section)




                                                                                                  Page 576 of 614
8. PHYSICAL PLACEMENT AND PRIVACY BOUNDARIES

Questions Considered:

    •   How is it ensured that cameras do not capture views into private homes?
    •   Are certain areas such as recreational spaces excluded from surveillance?

Summary of Responses:

In my professional opinion, camera placement is targeted based on documented operational needs, focusing
on infrastructure and areas with recurring issues. It is my understanding that cameras are not intended to
capture areas where there is a reasonable expectation of privacy, and certain recreational areas have been
excluded.

Which Attachments Give Detailed Answers:

    •   Public Works Verkada camera uses
    •   Camera Discussion (Placement Section)



9. PROCESS AND DECISION-MAKING

Questions Considered:

    •   Why was this system implemented without a public vote or broader community input?
    •   Why was this speci�ic vendor selected?
    •   Should decisions of this magnitude be subject to public approval?

Summary of Responses:

It is my understanding that the system was implemented after being discussed across multiple publicly
noticed meetings and formal Board action, including unanimous approval. The record indicates that the
decision was driven by operational needs identi�ied by staff. Vendor selection appears to have followed
established procurement processes, including state bid options. Future decisions regarding public input
are, in my view, a policy consideration for the Board.

Which Attachments Give Detailed Answers:

    •   Camera Discussion (Process section)
    •   Verkada Security and Privacy Resources

It is my opinion that the responses to the questions presented indicate that the system is being used in a
controlled and purpose-driven manner, with attention to legal compliance and operational need. At the
same time, I believe that the concerns raised highlight the value of establishing a formal, Town-wide policy
framework to further clarify governance, transparency, and acceptable-use moving forward.

In Public Service,


Stefen Wynn, M.P.A
ICMA-CM
Paonia Town Administrator & Treasurer

       o   If disabled locally, could it still be enabled or used by others in the future?

                   permissions to a third party as a user.

                   that identi�ies an individual. It would only be searching for an image that the
                   organization uploads for image recognition.

                           these settings disabled.
                         Org admin settings are going to be crucial for any alerts all other
                           users are view only with archive capability.

                           agencies would need to go to the user with that warrant.

                                            Department
 STAFF REPORT: VERKADA                                                                 04/20/2026




Public Safety Video Surveillance Program Report
1. Policy Overview

A formal policy governing the use of public safety video surveillance systems is in place within
the Police Department and was established prior to the implementation of surveillance cameras.
This ensures that all operations are conducted within defined legal, ethical, and procedural
standards.



2. Operational Objectives

The public safety video surveillance program supports the department’s mission through the
following objectives:

   •   Effective allocation and deployment of personnel
   •   Enhancement of public safety and security
   •   Improved situational awareness and response capabilities
   •   Monitor, detect, and respond to threats against critical infrastructure and important assets



3. System Access and Control

Access to the public safety video surveillance system is strictly limited to ensure security,
accountability, and proper use.

   •   Only the Chief of Police and the Patrol Sergeant are authorized to access and operate the
       video surveillance system.
   •   Department personnel seeking to view recorded video footage must submit a written
       request specifying the purpose and relevance of the footage.




                                                                                            Page 583 of 614
   •   All requests to view video must be directly related to and in furtherance of a criminal
       investigation.
   •   In exigent circumstances, a verbal request may be made to obtain immediate access;
       however, such requests must be documented in writing as soon as practicable following
       the incident.

These access controls safeguard system integrity, protect individual privacy, and ensure
compliance with departmental policy and applicable law.



4. Training Requirements

All department members authorized to operate or access public video surveillance systems are
required to complete appropriate training. This training includes:

   •   Proper use and operation of surveillance cameras
   •   Review of departmental policies and procedures
   •   Instruction on applicable state and federal laws related to video surveillance and privacy
       rights



5. Video Surveillance Audit

The Chief of Police, or an authorized designee, conducts an annual review of the public safety
video surveillance system. This review includes:

   •   Evaluation of system cost, benefits, and overall effectiveness
   •   Analysis of public safety issues addressed through surveillance
   •   Review of significant investigations or prosecutions supported by the system
   •   Identification of operational or administrative issues, including those related to training,
       discipline, or policy

All findings are formally documented and maintained. Any recommendations for improvements
in training or policy are addressed promptly.



6. Prohibited Activities

The use of public safety video surveillance systems is strictly regulated to prevent misuse. The
following activities are prohibited:

   •   Intentional invasion of individual privacy or monitoring areas where a reasonable
       expectation of privacy exists




                                                                                            Page 584 of 614
   •   Discriminatory or biased use based on characteristics such as race, ethnicity, national
       origin, religion, sex, sexual orientation, gender identity or expression, economic status,
       age, cultural background, or disability
   •   Use of surveillance equipment to harass, intimidate, or unfairly target individuals or
       groups



7. Authorized Uses of Surveillance Systems

Public safety video surveillance systems may be utilized for legitimate law enforcement
purposes, including:

   •   Preventing, deterring, and identifying criminal activity
   •   Monitoring areas with known gang or narcotics-related activity
   •   Responding to critical incidents
   •   Assisting in the identification, apprehension, and prosecution of offenders
   •   Documenting interactions between officers and the public to ensure accountability and
       protect rights
   •   Enhancing resources in a cost-effective manner
   •   Monitoring pedestrian and vehicular traffic activity



8. Conclusion

The public safety video surveillance program serves as a critical tool in supporting law
enforcement operations while maintaining accountability, transparency, and respect for
individual rights. Ongoing training, regular audits, and strict access controls ensure the system is
used effectively, responsibly, and in accordance with established policies.




                                                                                            Page 585 of 614
                                                                       Town of Paonia
                                                                               214 Grand Ave.
                                                                            Paonia, CO 81428
                                                                           O: (970) 527-4101
                                                                    Paonia@TownofPaonia.com


April 22, 2026
RE: Public Works use of Verkada Cameras


Public Works uses the cameras for the following uses: Public Works has user access
only. Director has access to all cameras. Water operators have access to both plants.
Public Works Supervisor has access but has never logged in or completed his profile.

Shop Cameras

   • Shop parking and storage security – historical theft and trespassing
   • Bulk water fill security – recent severe damages that resulted in court case and
     restitution. People rely on that water fill as their main source of drinking water.
     We need to protect the infrastructure.
   • Use the camera during the winter season to monitor snow fall and when to call
     out staff to plow.

Water Plant

   • Interior camera – operators can see inside the plant quicker than having to drive
     to the plant late at night. The plant has a built in alarm system that will call the on
     call operator. However, some of these alarms can be faulty. So, the internal
     camera helps to trouble shoot actual emergencies as well as helps the operator
     determine when and how something may have gone wrong when they are not
     there.
   • External camera – supplies security to a secure and private location. The water
     plant is on private property. Historical trespassing issues of citizens and
     unauthorized individuals. We also use them to monitor contractor traffic




                                                                                  Page 586 of 614
Wastewater Plant

   • Historical events of aerators malfunctioning and blowing apart. Cameras supply a
     timeline of how long they have been down if this event occurs. PW has replaced
     some aerators with a newer model but we still have the pontoon style in place.
     The cameras assist in trouble shooting and timeline of malfunction.
   • Also, historical abuse of trespassing by citizens as well as illegal dumping.

Park

   • PW will view cameras after property damage has occurred. Multiple times the
     fence has been ran over or broken, the large gazebo structure is vandalized with
     obscene drawings and language and the grills being used as toilets.
   • There is/was high criminal activity between the skate park and the PW park shed
   • Illegal dumping at the dumpsters and at the skate park.

Front Town Hall

   • Public Works has used this camera when an incident was reported involving an
     alleged drunk driver almost hitting a PW employee. Suspect drove in wrong lane
     of traffic from 3rd St to 2nd street and about hit the employee head on.


Public Works reviews footage only after suspicious behavior or incidents occur.

Reasons we have used the cameras

    Verifying cleaning crews have come on weekends
    Checking Town Hall when alarms have gone off after hours
    When working in ofϐice late and waiting for people who were using the common room after
     ofϐice hours, I was able to see when they arrived to go see if they needed anything, also used
     for determining when event was over so I could clear and lock up the building when empty,
     while staying secure in the ofϐice.
    Reviewing for out of the norm customer/employee interactions or to verify
     customer/employee complaints
    Verifying beginning and ending of Board Room rentals and the securing of Town Hall doors
    At least one instance of vandalism of town property has been caught on camera.
    Verifying payment arrangement details or minutes
Reasons I would continue to use them:

      Town Hall staff handles money, including cash for permits and utility bills
      Building/staff safety after hours
      Though not the norm, there have been instances of accusations/verbal threats/threatening
       behavior by customers towards front ofϐice staff multiple times during my time working here.
       I feel being able to document those instances are important for whatever action needs to be
       taken.
      We have left the Town Hall bathrooms open to the public during certain Town events.

Today when I wrote this, I had to access the Verkada Dash Board to see which cameras I have viewer
access to. I have the cameras at Town Hall and at the Town Shop and Bulk Water Fill station. Other
than when they were ϐirst installed and I looked to see what views I had, I only access the cameras
when I need to.

All,

Please see my responses to the questions in italics below.

       Legal Compliance and Privacy Protections
            Are live camera feeds accessible in a manner that complies with
            Colorado law, including warrant requirements? (I’m assuming that this
            concern is for publicly available live feeds, not feeds available to
            government employees)
                 Cameras may be but are not required to be live feed, i.e. active
                 surveillance. Cameras that record for later review are considered passive
                 surveillance. Most of the surveillance requirements in Colorado stem from
                 criminal code and an individual’s right to privacy, and there is no
                 differentiation between active and passive surveillance. Colorado allows
                 cameras in public areas as long as they are not recording an area where
                 someone has a “Reasonable Expectation of Privacy,” generally considered
                 as changing rooms, bathrooms, inside personal residence, etc…

                 Warrants are not required for access/use of public surveillance whether
                 active or passive. Note: SB26-071, if passed, would require a warrant to
                 access facial recognition systems data.


               Does audio recording violate federal laws such as the Electronic
               Communications Privacy Act or wiretap statutes?
                 Audio recording is allowed under the same rules as video in public areas
                 where there is no reasonable expectation of privacy. Colorado goes a step
                 further and allows audio recording in non-public areas under a one-party
                 consent rule, which means that it legal to audio record in non-public areas
                 as long as one party involved in a conversation is aware the conversation is
                 being recorded and consents to the recording.

                 The ECPA is a prohibition on certain interception, access, or disclosure of
                 wire, oral, and electronic communications (email, phone calls, stored
                 data) by limiting surveillance, warrants for real-time wiretapping,




                                                                                                    Page 590 of 614
              protecting stored data, and controlling the process for law enforcement
              access. It does not apply to the Town’s public surveillance cameras.


            How does single party consent law in Colorado apply to these
            recordings?
              See answer above.

              Audio recording is allowed under the same rules as video in public areas
              where there is no reasonable expectation of privacy. Colorado goes a step
              further and allows audio recording in non-public areas under a one-party
              consent rule, which means that it legal to audio record in non-public areas
              as long as one party involved in a conversation is aware the conversation is
              being recorded and consents to the recording.


            What is the legal standing of the end user agreement and associated
            terms?
              Verkada’s end user agreement is a contract that governs the purchase and
              use of Verkada’s products, including software, hardware, product
              features, documentation, and all modification, updates, and upgrades
              thereto. The Town, as the end user, is bound by the terms and conditions
              stated in the contract as it is in any other third-party contract for goods
              and/or services.



I will continuously monitor legislative changes to ensure ongoing compliance with the
current system. I understand these issues can be complex, and am happy to discuss
further.

V/r

-Clay

How Verkada Helps Organizations Responsibly Use Facial Recognition Technology (FRT)
Facial recognition technology (FRT) is becoming ubiquitous in our lives today, and demand
for its application is growing. This technology powers our phone access, our lobbies, and
airport check-ins. Today, there is no longer a question of living without FRT, but rather how
do we use this technology responsibly and in a way that helps maintain our privacy.

We think about this delicate balance every day at Verkada, and it's what drives us to find new,
innovative ways to help our customers. It is what has inspired our privacy-focused controls
and features – from Person of Interest Only Face Search, which helps companies more tightly
control when and how they use FRT in alignment with local privacy requirements, to Face
Blur, which enables live monitoring while respecting individual privacy.

                          STAFF REPORT




AGENDA ITEM:         Presentation of Draft Ordinance by Trustees Hunter and Majorado to establish
                     requirements and regulations governing the installation and operation of Town
                     security camera for the purpose of public discussion and potential edits.
SUBMITTED BY:        Trustees Hunter and Majorado


DATE:                April 14, 2026


BACKGROUND:          We recognize that video surveillance technology can serve legitimate public
                     safety and property protection purposes when deployed responsibly and
                     transparently. We also recognize the potential for abuse with regard to facial
                     recognition, license plate reading, and immigration enforcement, among other
                     concerns. We would like to formally address the absence of public policy in this
                     regard in a way that allows the intended use of security cameras at Town
                     facilities while strictly prohibiting uses that were not intended.




BUDGET:              Staff time



                The Draft Ordinance is NOT up for adoption at this time. Possible motions
RECOMMENDATION: include edits to the Draft and directing Staff for further edits / review.




ATTACHMENT:          Draft Ordinance




                                                                                             Page 595 of 614
                           TOWN OF PAONIA, COLORADO
                        DELTA COUNTY, STATE OF COLORADO

                                ORDINANCE NO. ___-2026

AN ORDINANCE OF THE BOARD OF TRUSTEES OF THE TOWN OF PAONIA, COLORADO,
 ESTABLISHING REQUIREMENTS AND REGULATIONS GOVERNING THE INSTALLATION
   AND OPERATION OF GOVERNMENT VIDEO SURVEILLANCE SYSTEMS ON TOWN-
    OWNED OR TOWN-CONTROLLED PROPERTY; REQUIRING THE ADOPTION AND
     MAINTENANCE OF A PUBLIC USE POLICY; ESTABLISHING RESTRICTIONS ON
FOOTAGE RETENTION, ACCESS, AND DISCLOSURE; PROVIDING TRANSPARENCY AND
    ACCOUNTABILITY MECHANISMS; AND PROVIDING PENALTIES FOR VIOLATION
                                THEREOF.


RECITALS
WHEREAS, the Board of Trustees of the Town of Paonia ("Board") recognizes that video
surveillance technology can serve legitimate public safety and property protection purposes when
deployed responsibly and transparently; and


WHEREAS, the Board also recognizes that government video surveillance of public spaces
implicates the privacy interests of residents, visitors, and employees, and that such interests
deserve legal protection; and


WHEREAS, the Board finds that the absence of a publicly available use policy, clear retention
limits, and meaningful oversight creates unacceptable risks of misuse and erodes public trust;
and


WHEREAS, the Federal Trade Commission has taken enforcement action against Verkada, Inc.
relating to data security and access control failures, underscoring the importance of robust
governance requirements for cloud-connected surveillance systems; and


WHEREAS, the Board of Trustees has authority under Article XX of the Colorado Constitution
and Title 31 of the Colorado Revised Statutes to regulate matters of local concern, including the
use of technology by Town government; and


WHEREAS, the Board finds it in the best interest of the health, safety, and welfare of the citizens
of Paonia to establish clear, enforceable standards governing government video surveillance;


NOW, THEREFORE, BE IT ORDAINED BY THE BOARD OF TRUSTEES OF THE TOWN OF
PAONIA, COLORADO, AS FOLLOWS:



Section 1. Purpose and Intent




                                                                                          Page 596 of 614
The purpose of this Ordinance is to establish a comprehensive regulatory framework governing
the installation, operation, access, retention, and oversight of video surveillance systems operated
by or on behalf of the Town of Paonia. This Ordinance is intended to:

   •   Authorize the regulated use of video surveillance for legitimate public safety and
       property protection purposes;
   •   Protect the privacy and civil liberties of all persons in spaces subject to Town
       surveillance;
   •   Ensure transparency through mandatory public disclosure of surveillance policies and
       camera locations;
   •   Establish meaningful oversight, accountability, and enforcement mechanisms;
   •   Prevent mission creep, unauthorized access, and indiscriminate monitoring of residents.


Section 2. Definitions


As used in this Ordinance, the following terms shall have the meanings set forth below:

   •   "Authorized Purpose" means any of the purposes enumerated in Section 4 of this
       Ordinance for which the Town may operate a surveillance camera.
   •   "Designee" means any Town employee, officer, or contractor authorized by the Town
       Administrator to access surveillance footage.
   •   "Footage" means any video, audio, image, or metadata recorded, stored, or transmitted
       by a surveillance camera system, whether stored locally or in a cloud-based
       environment.
   •   "Privacy Impact Assessment" or "PIA" means a documented analysis evaluating the
       privacy risks of a proposed surveillance installation or material system change, as further
       defined in Section 6.
   •   "Sensitive Location" means any area where individuals have a heightened expectation of
       privacy, including but not limited to restrooms, locker rooms, changing areas, and interior
       spaces not regularly open to the public.
   •   "Surveillance Camera" means any fixed or mobile electronic device capable of capturing,
       recording, transmitting, or storing video images of persons or property in real time or on
       a continuous or periodic basis, including but not limited to IP cameras, network-
       connected cameras, and cloud-integrated camera systems.
   •   "Surveillance System" means one or more surveillance cameras operated together,
       including associated software platforms, storage infrastructure, and cloud service
       components.
   •   "Town" means the Town of Paonia, Colorado, including all its departments, officers,
       employees, and contractors acting in their official capacity.
   •   "Use Policy" means the Town of Paonia Surveillance Camera Use Policy adopted
       pursuant to Section 5 of this Ordinance.


Section 3. Scope and Applicability




                                                                                           Page 597 of 614
This Ordinance applies to all surveillance cameras and surveillance systems owned, leased,
operated, or controlled by the Town of Paonia, regardless of where such systems are located or
how they are funded. This Ordinance does not apply to:

   •   Body-worn cameras operated by law enforcement pursuant to a separate policy;
   •   Temporary recording equipment used in connection with a specific, documented
       investigation pursuant to a lawful warrant or court order;
   •   Cameras operated by private parties on private property not under Town control, except
       where the Town has contractual access to footage from such systems.


Section 4. Authorized Purposes


The Town may install and operate surveillance cameras only for the following Authorized
Purposes:

   •   Protection of Town-owned facilities, buildings, infrastructure, and equipment from
       vandalism, theft, or unauthorized access;
   •   Monitoring of Town-owned parking facilities, public parks, and recreational areas for
       public safety;
   •   Deterrence and documentation of criminal activity in areas that have experienced
       documented incidents;
   •   Protection of employees and members of the public in Town facilities;
   •   Traffic monitoring at public intersections or rights-of-way where authorized by separate
       resolution.

Surveillance cameras shall not be operated for any purpose not listed above.


Section 5. Mandatory Use Policy


(a) No surveillance camera or surveillance system subject to this Ordinance shall be operated
unless and until the Board of Trustees has adopted, by resolution, a written Surveillance Camera
Use Policy ("Use Policy") that complies with the requirements of this Section.


(b) The Use Policy shall address, at minimum, the following subjects:

   •   A complete inventory of all surveillance cameras operated by the Town, including their
       locations, fields of view, and Authorized Purpose;
   •   The categories of personnel authorized to access live or recorded footage, and under
       what circumstances;
   •   The maximum retention period for footage, which shall not exceed ninety (90) days for
       routine recordings absent an active investigation, legal hold, or insurance claim;




                                                                                         Page 598 of 614
   •   The circumstances under which footage may be shared with law enforcement, other
       governmental agencies, or third parties, and the applicable legal standards;
   •   Prohibitions on use of footage for facial recognition, license plate reader aggregation, or
       behavioral analytics absent explicit Board authorization by ordinance;
   •   A procedure for receiving and responding to public records requests for footage
       consistent with the Colorado Open Records Act (C.R.S. § 24-72-201 et seq.);
   •   A procedure for receiving and investigating complaints regarding alleged misuse of
       surveillance systems;
   •   Training requirements for all personnel with access to surveillance footage;
   •   Penalties for unauthorized access or misuse of footage.

(c) The Use Policy shall be:

   •   Posted in a prominent location on the Town's official website;
   •   Made available for public inspection at Town Hall during normal business hours;
   •   Reviewed and re-adopted or updated at least once every two (2) years.

(d) Any material amendment to the Use Policy shall require approval by the Board of Trustees at
a public meeting with no less than fourteen (14) days' advance public notice.


Section 6. Privacy Impact Assessment


(a) Prior to installing any new surveillance camera or materially expanding or relocating an
existing system, the Town Administrator or law enforcement designee shall prepare and submit
to the Board of Trustees a written Privacy Impact Assessment ("PIA").


(b) A PIA shall include:

   •   A description of the proposed installation, including location, field of view, and technical
       specifications;
   •   The Authorized Purpose(s) served by the proposed installation;
   •   An assessment of the privacy interests affected and the proportionality of surveillance to
       the asserted need;
   •   An analysis of less privacy-invasive alternatives considered;
   •   A description of physical and cybersecurity measures to protect footage from
       unauthorized access;
   •   The identity and contractual obligations of any third-party vendor providing hardware,
       software, or cloud storage services.

(c) The Board of Trustees shall approve the PIA at a public meeting before any new installation
may proceed. Approval may be conditioned on additional safeguards.




                                                                                           Page 599 of 614
(d) For surveillance cameras in operation as of the effective date of this Ordinance, the Town
Administrator or law enforcement designee shall prepare and submit a PIA to the Board within
ninety (90) days of adoption. During this period, existing cameras may continue interim operation
as permitted by Section 11.


Section 7. Location Restrictions


Surveillance cameras shall not be installed or operated in the following locations:

   •   Any Sensitive Location as defined in Section 2;
   •   Any location where the primary or predominant field of view captures the interior of a
       private residence;
   •   Any location where the camera would primarily capture lawful activity on private
       property;
   •   Any location not otherwise authorized by an approved PIA and the Use Policy.

Cameras shall be positioned to minimize incidental capture of areas beyond the scope of the
Authorized Purpose, and shall be physically adjusted or shielded as needed to comply with this
Section.

Section 8. Access, Security, and Data Protection


(a) Access to live or recorded footage shall be limited to the Town Administrator, authorized law
enforcement agencies acting pursuant to a lawful legal process, and Designees specifically
authorized in the Use Policy.


(b) All access to footage shall be logged in a written or electronic access log recording the date,
time, identity of the person accessing footage, the footage accessed, and the stated purpose. The
access log shall be treated as a public record subject to inspection upon request.


(c) The Town shall implement reasonable physical and cybersecurity safeguards to prevent
unauthorized access to footage, including password protection, access controls, and encryption
in transit and at rest for cloud-stored footage.


(d) In the event of a security breach or unauthorized access to footage, the Town Administrator
or law enforcement designee shall notify the Board of Trustees and the affected parties, to the
extent identifiable, within seventy-two (72) hours of discovery.


(e) Cloud storage and software vendors shall be contractually required to comply with the data
security and access requirements of this Ordinance and shall not use footage for any commercial
purpose, including advertising, analytics, or product development.


Section 9. Oversight and Reporting




                                                                                          Page 600 of 614
(a) The Town Administrator or law enforcement designee shall submit an annual written report
to the Board of Trustees at the first regular meeting of each calendar year, addressing:

   •   The current number and location of surveillance cameras in operation;
   •   A summary of all access log entries for the prior year, including the number of instances
       of footage access and the categories of purpose;
   •   Any complaints received regarding surveillance system use and their disposition;
   •   Any security incidents or unauthorized access events;
   •   Recommendations for system changes, policy updates, or decommissioning of cameras.

(b) The annual report shall be made publicly available on the Town's website.


(c) The Board of Trustees may, at any time, by majority vote, order an audit of any aspect of the
surveillance program, including inspection of the access log, vendor contracts, and security
configurations.


Section 10. Prohibited Uses


Notwithstanding any other provision of this Ordinance or the Use Policy, Town surveillance
cameras shall not be used for:

   •   Facial recognition or biometric identification, unless explicitly authorized by a separate
       ordinance adopted by the Board following a public hearing;
   •   License plate reader data aggregation or tracking of individual vehicle movements over
       time;
   •   Real-time or retrospective tracking of specific individuals based on race, ethnicity,
       religion, national origin, political affiliation, sexual orientation, or other protected
       characteristics;
   •   Sharing footage with federal immigration enforcement authorities without a valid court
       order;
   •   Any commercial purpose, including sale or licensing of footage to third parties.


Section 11. Interim Operation and Compliance Deadline for Existing Systems


All surveillance cameras and surveillance systems in operation as of the effective date of this
Ordinance shall be brought into full compliance with this Ordinance within one hundred eighty
(180) days of the effective date (the “Compliance Deadline”). During the interim period between
the effective date and the Compliance Deadline, existing cameras may continue to operate solely
for the Authorized Purposes enumerated in Section 4, subject to the access, security, prohibited
use, and public notice requirements of Sections 7 through 10. Continued interim operation does
not relieve the Town of its obligation to complete Privacy Impact Assessments and adopt a Use
Policy within the timeframes set forth in Sections 5 and 6. Any camera or system not brought into




                                                                                          Page 601 of 614
full compliance by the Compliance Deadline shall be suspended from operation by the Town until
compliance is achieved, or decommissioned if compliance cannot be achieved within an
additional thirty (30) days.


Section 12. Enforcement and Penalties


(a) Any Town officer or employee who knowingly accesses, uses, shares, or retains footage in
violation of this Ordinance shall be subject to disciplinary action up to and including termination,
in addition to any penalties provided by applicable law.


(b) Any person who believes the Town has violated this Ordinance may file a written complaint
with the Town Clerk. The Town Administrator or law enforcement designee shall investigate and
respond in writing within thirty (30) days.


(c) Violations of this Ordinance by a vendor or contractor may result in termination of the
applicable contract.


Section 13. Severability


If any provision of this Ordinance or its application to any person or circumstance is held invalid
or unconstitutional by a court of competent jurisdiction, the remainder of the Ordinance and its
application to other persons and circumstances shall not be affected.


Section 14. Effective Date


This Ordinance shall take effect thirty (30) days following final passage and publication as
required by C.R.S. § 31-16-103.




INTRODUCED, READ, APPROVED ON FIRST READING, AND ORDERED PUBLISHED this

                    Consideration of Task Order from SGM to Re-purpose
                    Temporary Tank to Raw Water Storage Tank
^hD/ddz͗
                    Stefen Wynn, Town Administrator & Treasurer
d͗
                    April 24, 2026
<'ZKhE͗         The Town of Paonia previously installed a temporary potable water storage tank to
                    support operations during the 2MG tank relining project. With that project nearing
                    completion, staff has evaluated opportunities to repurpose existing infrastructure in a cost
                    effective and operationally beneficial manner. SGM has provided a proposal to convert
                    the temporary potable water tank into a permanent raw water storage tank. As outlined in
                    the attached letter proposal, this approach leverages the fact that the tank was already
                    designed and approved under potable water standards, including overflow and venting
                    requirements. Following coordination with CDPHE, SGM has indicated that there are no
                    specific design criteria governing raw water storage tanks. As a result, the conversion
                    can proceed with a streamlined process. SGM will prepare a courtesy submittal to
                    CDPHE to formally document the change in use, ensuring the tank is incorporated into
                    the Town’s water system inventory and avoiding potential concerns during future sanitary
                    surveys. The scope of work includes preparation of a revised site plan reflecting new inlet
                    and outlet connections to the raw water system, abandonment of existing potable
                    connections, photographic documentation of modifications, and a memorandum
                    describing the completed work. Repurposing the tank provides an opportunity to enhance
                    system flexibility, improve raw water management, and maximize the value of an existing
                    asset without the need for full decommissioning.


h'd͗
                    60-50-20: $10,000 for SGM Design work (Decision Tonight)
                    60-50-70: $212,300 to purchase temporary tank (Decision Later)

                    RECOMMENDED MOTION:
ZKDDEd/KE͗ I move to approve the task order with SGM in an amount not to exceed
                    $10,000 for design and permitting services to convert the temporary
                    potable water tank into a permanent raw water storage tank, and to
                    authorize the Town Administrator and Treasurer to execute all necessary
                    documents.

Stefen Wynn

RE:    Temporary Potable Tank to Permanent Raw Water Storage Tank

Dear Stefen:

This letter presents SGM’s proposed scope of services related to the design and permitting for changing
the current temporary potable water tank into a permanent raw water storage tank.

PROPOSED SGM SCOPE OF WORK
After multiple discussions with CDPHE, we present the following. CDPHE doesn’t have any design
criteria for raw water storage tanks allowing the Town to proceed relatively unchecked. Given that the
temporary potable water storage tank was already approved through CDPHE with proper overflow and
vent calculations, SGM will provide CDPHE a “courtesy” submittal for switching the tank from potable use
to raw water storage. This ensures the tank becomes part of the inventory and doesn’t create any red
flags during sanitary surveys performed by CDPHE. This submittal will contain new site plan with new
inlet and outlet connections to the raw water line, and abandonment/disconnection of current inlet and
outlet piping from the potable line. We’ll also provide pictures of the new connections and abandonment
of the potable water connections along with a memo describing the whole project.

FEE PROPOSAL

We estimate fees at $10,000 to complete this work. Changes in scope requested by the Town or due to
circumstances beyond SGM’s control may require a contract Change Order. SGM will bring any out-of-
scope items to the Town’s attention as they arise and seek authorization to proceed in advance.

If you have questions or concerns that you would like to discuss I can be reached at 970-384-9024.
Thank you.


Sincerely,



Brandyn Bair, PE
Wastewater Team Leader

                  Consideration of Change Order #3 for KLM Engineering


^hD/ddz͗
                  Stefen Wynn, Town Administrator & Treasurer
d͗
                  April 24, 2026
<'ZKhE͗       The Town of Paonia is currently undertaking the 2MG finished water storage tank
                  relining project, which includes coating inspection services performed by KLM
                  Engineering, Inc. The original agreement established a not to exceed amount of
                  $97,860.00 for inspection services. Due to project delays and extended
                  construction duration associated with the contractor’s performance, additional
                  inspection time has been required to maintain appropriate oversight and ensure
                  compliance with coating specifications and industry standards. Change Order #1
                  and Change Order #2 were previously approved to account for these extended
                  inspection periods. Change Order #3 reflects an additional one week of AMPP
                  coating inspection services billed on a time and materials basis, including
                  mobilization, through April 13, 2026, at a daily rate of $1,568.00, for a total of
                  $26,700.00.Approval of this change order increases the total not to exceed
                  contract amount with KLM Engineering to $190,423.00.It should be noted that a
                  final change order from KLM Engineering is anticipated and will be presented to
                  the Board at the May 12, 2026 meeting. This final change order will reflect total
                  inspection costs through substantial completion of the project.


h'd͗           Engineer's POC: $2,181,300; Total Costs Incurred to Date: $1,622,959 (incl. MPC
                  Disbursement 4.28.26); Remaining Obligated Under Existing Contracts: $279,832;
                  Projected total Cost (current commitments): $1,902,791; Remaining Margin (after CO
                  #3): approx. $278,300 (previously Approx. $305,000).

                  RECOMMENDED MOTION:
ZKDDEd/KE͗
                  I move to approve Change Order #3 for KLM Engineering in the
                  amount of $26,700.00, increasing the total not to exceed contract
                  amount to $190,423.00, and authorize the Town Administrator to
                  execute the agreement.

                    TOWN OF PAONIA
                    BOARD OF TRUSTEES MEETING
                    MAYOR REPORT

AGENDA ITEM:    Consideration of Not Charging for Flower Pots on the Sidewalk for
                Business Use Permit
SUBMITTED BY:   Mayor Smith

DATE:           April 28, 2026

BACKGROUND:     It recently came to my attention that businesses on Grand Ave. are interested
                in placing freestanding flowerpots/planters (those that are of adequate size
                to be placed directly on the sidewalk) in front of their businesses. However,
                business owners are hesitant to do so because the business use of the
                sidewalk permit has historically charged the standard $25 fee for each
                flowerpot/planter.

                The following Town Code requires an annual payment for each item placed
                on the public sidewalk for business or advertising purposes. This can be
                equated with a business “renting” public space to conduct their private
                business.

                Sec. 11-1-30. - Use of public sidewalks for business purposes; permit.

                (a) Any legal entity, person, group, business or nonprofit organization
                wishing to utilize a portion of the Town sidewalk for business or advertising
                purposes may do so in the areas of Town zoned C-1 and C-2, if the following
                criteria are met:

                        (1) The merchandise, sign, sandwich board (no larger than 36" x 36"
                        x 36"), newspaper box, real estate brochure box, bench, table or
                        chairs are moveable and not attached to the sidewalk.

                        (2) Any person utilizing the sidewalk for these business purposes
                        shall maintain the sidewalk and premises in good and safe condition
                        and shall preserve a minimum of thirty-six (36) inches of continuous
                        open walkway and passages.

                        (3) The legal entity, person, group, business, or nonprofit
                        organization wishing to use a Town sidewalk for allowed business
                        purposes may at any time of the year apply for said use. After
                        coordinating the location of the item with the Town Administrator
                        and paying the yearly administrative fee set by the Town
                        Administrator, the Town will issue a permit for the item to be placed
                        only on the sidewalk in front of the business. Existing permits may




                                                                                         Page 609 of 614
                  be renewed during the month of June each year. One (1) permit is
                  required per item per location.

          (b) The Town may revoke or suspend a permit or the rights granted herein
          as it deems appropriate in its sole discretion. No vested right to use the
          Town sidewalk for personal or business purposes shall be obtained.

          (c) One-day events utilizing the sidewalk not exceeding a total of five (5)
          events per year shall not violate this Section requiring a permit, provided
          that the criteria set forth in Paragraphs (a)(1) and (2) above are satisfied.

          Beatification of our downtown is greatly enhanced by the inclusion of
          flowerpots/planters and provides a welcoming atmosphere for all that visit
          our downtown shopping district. Consequently, I would like to propose
          direction from the Board to staff to exempt individual, freestanding
          flowerpots/planters from the $25 individual assessment with the caveat
          that no such flowerpot/planter can be used to hold a sign advertising any
          business, display merchandise, etc. This would clearly constitute a
          “business” use. In addition, it will be the responsibility of the business
          owner(s) to care for their respective planters throughout the growing
          season.

          The “Business Use of Public Sidewalk” permit applications are generally
          submitted in June. If this exemption is approved by the Trustees, those
          businesses that may have already submitted their annual permit and
          included payment for a flowerpot/planter would be reimbursed for the $25
          fee. A copy of the Town’s Permit application is provided as Attachment A.

          Businesses will still need to identify that they are planning to have a
          flowerpot/planter on the sidewalk when they submit the annual permit
          application; the fee would be waived for the flowerpot(s)/planter(s).

          In addition, a permit application will still be required from a business even if
          the owner is only planning to add a flowerpot/planter to the sidewalk in
          front of their business. This is still necessary because of the “terms and
          conditions” provided in the permit application that must be agreed to in
          order to use the public sidewalk.

          Please note that the preexisting concrete downtown planters* that have
          been designed and planted by a citizen volunteer (Shane Smith) for the last
          two years will be planted by him again this year and the Town will be
          watering these planters throughout the growing season.

          *This does not include the planters managed by OakStar and UB banks.
BUDGET:   Loss of $25 per on-sidewalk flowerpot/planter




                                                                                  Page 610 of 614
RECOMMENDATION:   Make a motion to exempt free-standing flowerpots/planters placed on the
                  sidewalk and managed by individual businesses from the $25 “business use of
                  the sidewalk” fee.
